Creating human exclusive things, not to label the different, but to differentiate the label, a discrimination on the surroundings. A delusion of plagued beings, to keep them plaguing. That the brevity of life makes humans stickier, less variable. On average we take ever more time to be "formed"? Formed as in formados, tipo estudados licenciados prontos a contribuir para a máquina.

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: The Decline of the Inventor and Misunderstanding Language
Philosophy.content: "Everyone's got a story to tell, Everyone knows about it." And this simulacra and simulation is to get worse, for we are mass producing designers and those alike. Inventor is a degrading word these days. There was a time where growing up to be an inventor was a thing. And so much distance we seem to have put on that time, the train is accelerating, it's ever harder to stop it. I should make a pray for Nietzsche man, two bus trips where this man's words have made my thought alive. Thanks my brotha. And you welcome, next one in line. It doesn't cease to disappoint. It's like I'm catching nothing, but then an opening opens and holy moly am I running through that door. There should be such a thing as silence exposure therapy. Again it seems, like me and Nietzsche, that first one dominates language before one revolts against it. In this sense one should look for misunderstanding the language of another, or else he would be limited to the meaning of words. We gotta use language to go beyond it.

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: Unity of Words and the Power of Ideas
Philosophy.content: "The unity of the word does not guarantee the unity of the thing". This can be screwed up real badly by those who assigned words to meanings and those who made synonyms. They imposed similarities in the world, unconsciously absorbed by the all-open babies. Sometimes as humans we forget that language is only a way of understanding. Like we can legit have misconceptions so engrained that few notice it that stem from calling different things the same. This is no little theme. Study this point carefully. "Strong belief proves only its own strength, not the truth of what it believes." The one electron Universe. The one will Universe. The world as will and representation.

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: Bounded by Space and Time
Philosophy.content: That today one isn't anymore bounded to space and time, or has this possibility. And what a concept it is, to be fluid, to be mobile in the great hall of humanity. Space and time have to be honored as the greatest binders one faces in his life. Going all the way with this idea of a raw world of no idea whatsoever, and then coming back, once again the hardest part, and molding the world to your truth driven built will. Reaction as progress, he points out, as in even if people's ideas aren't correct, they tell you what is wrong so that you can screw the balance in the right direction. Someone talking greatly about metaphysics can be an hint that too weak is science right now. For eternal glory is seized through today's glory. That man only finds purpose with illogical thought, with impurity of thought. Only when selfishly focusing on individual things can one escape from the overarching numbness of mankind's direction.

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: Daring to Go Far and the Beauty of Silence
Philosophy.content: Expresses that feeling that "todo eu sou uma força que me escapa". As in when one dares advance to new territories, when one gives in to the dark side, one risks getting stuck and not coming back. Life as going as further as you can and earning the rewards when coming back. And those that keep going, those that lose themselves to their spirit. Those condemned to fulfill their destiny. For life naturally divides its spirits in half: those who preferred to hide from their potential, and those condemned to become its slave. To be the slave of greatness or the master of littleness. And with eye-opening suspicion comes faith, for a sane homeostatic being does not risk himself so wide. Fighting against nature, daring to see oneself as more real than nature. It's so beautiful to hear the silence for a moment, when all you've been hearing was nothing. A blank day becomes peace. For whom silence is craziness, and for whom silence is peace. It's cool and dandy to let digest, but honestly I like the idea of forcefeeding, I like the idea of food for thought being shoved up my mouth down and back. Like vomiting but putting more in there. That everything poets ever did was creating into the poem what they wanted to have found but couldn't.

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: Bound Spirits and the Separation from Virtues
Philosophy.content: That free spirits were bound spirits, that a great separation turned them otherwise; they were bound to the external things that served them, that were good. And how can we condemn the bound spirits? For we bounded them, and hardly gave them life without that to which they were bound to. Not many planted the seed for their release. Most are themselves bound spirits, so how could they? Hence the feeling of betrayal for the bound of the pão nosso de cada dia. The separation of one with his virtues, his feats, his ways, so that one allows himself to own himself. Most people bother doing the proof of their propositions. They haven't proven to themselves that which they think about themselves. "Straining off everything of the coincidental." That is clarity. Como espremer um fruto para obter uma gota of the essence. Espreme o dia que tiveste hoje. Só sai água? É oco? I look around my bedroom, and why do we possess books to occupy shelves, books that we only used as bound spirits, to school, presents. We like to occupy space for some reason. So much dead space, so much dead time. We humans lack aliveness. So much dead thought. So many dead words.

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: Decadence, Taming the Beast, and Morality
Philosophy.content: Thinking of these things terrifies a bit, makes you afraid to go on with your life, just a tiny bit, you know? It pauses you on you. Ultimately Nietzsche goes back to the decadence concept. That he is fighting those who lost their smell, o seu faro, those who faded. We should shine hard, but the system wants us to fade as individuals. It makes us feel ashamed of shining so bright. It takes ease at us taking it easy. It takes ease at us not wanting to become ourselves. And ultimately this is what the book is about, how one becomes what one is. A bunch of yes-saying neutral colors. The world in essence becoming more black and white. No more radiation whether good or bad. Funnelling individuals down a pipe into a pool of everyone. Humanity misunderstanding itself. No longer are there villains and heroes. Each is different in their own way. Denying life, he dares say. Denying elements of human nature, he conjures, aggression, competition. Selfless beings unable to tune themselves with the frequency of their instincts. Playing around with ideas such as souls to ashame the body, the only soul you ever touched! Letting existence be handled by a world that is not ours, the divine. Amenização. Inclusion as stealing, not just stealing, that would be euphemism, it's taking away. Wrapping around the spirit. Calming the beast. Literally, oh my god, perfect fucking analogy: it's caging a tiger. It's putting a tiger in a zoo, feeding him ração. That's it right there. Devoiding him of purpose, it's castration, domar tigres que quando chegaram eram indomáveis. Isn't that what we are doing to children? And why even though they are relentless and unforgiving and authentic, they then end up with social anxiety and stuff? No baby thinks oh I'm doing too much noise. Should a baby never fall on his own risk? That's a question worth thinking about. Should you protect your baby to the point he doesn't take any risks? Of course not, that's stripping him of his learning, of his experience. Free spirits then lack today, for they have to free themselves, and few do. Shadow. Don't deny the meat that makes you. The fiber within you.

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: Proxies, Hope, and Escaping Reality
Philosophy.content: Meaning the harsh truth of things then takes that weight off of you. That people search for proxies, come up with concepts to help them alleviate the impregnable reality of what is their life. They wanna escape it, transcend them, find in the unreal that which discontented them in the real. They want to replace every real object from a piece of the world of ideas. They wanna rebuild the world because they can't handle it. They search for gaps, they see not the things but the shadows; that's why they see amazing miracles, like shadows faster than light. They want to add, they haven't yet realized the value of subtracting, a la Baudrillard. They find all that they can have not enough. They miss the very point. They play gambles in the world of possibility, their ignorance the reason for their hope. They use more what they don't know than what they do. If life was a long set of stairs, they spend all their time looking up, never to see that which is sure. It is as if they almost refuse to believe that which is in front of them, they refuse to believe in the tools they are handed. Believing in them is a choice, and a necessary one. They want to find all higher purposes so as to make their earthly existence have none. They make for themselves the bed that alleviates the pressure of there being things that can be done and they can do. A self-denial of sorts. A self-inhibition, prohibition. The opposite of selfism; the safer "don't believe what you don't see", so see what isn't there to rather believe in it. Instead of believing in things they don't yet see, but see possible, and reasons why, they prefer to believe in things that they think they see and that are by their nature not possible in the same way, so as to protect oneself from the angst of action.

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: Weakness and the Need to Empower
Philosophy.content: The most terrible of the consequences of this negation of the meat on the plate is the hopelessness and powerless feel that it can bring, the spreading, disease-like spreading of this feel. When the good man sides with the weak and sick, and doesn't know better, cause better is foreign. When weakness has penetrated so surely that one doesn't know another way. No soldier knows at all times no battle has been lost, and no such thing exists that can't be changed at your volition. Why am I a destiny. Future assured, future confirmed, I am in charge, forever in control. There is always a way. Charles de Gaulle type discourse. Empower yourself.

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: The Overhuman, Evil, and Seeing Reality
Philosophy.content: What he is concerned is that the highest humans would consider his overman devil. So the highest humans, he thinks, should have a dark side. Perhaps they first rose because of the good, then despite it. Those that show dark side early on don't get fed enough, they get fed enough to get them hanging on life by a thread, the system of good wants to hold them down. Perhaps not even eliminate them, but just hold them down so that their reasoning works: that the bad ones end up weak because they are bad, instead of they end up weak because others wanted so. And so again comes the concept of the loner untrusting of no one awake. Using the system to destroy it. And just like in that game which inspired me this idea, the game INSIDE, and the best part of it all is when the character shuts down your control over him. When will you free yourself from the controller? The overhuman is then seeing reality for what it is. The bad parts too. What goodness ends up doing is filtering, lying. The overhuman, in other words, doesn't shy away from evil; he is open to the possibility that the world is in essence unfair and ruthless, like some fragments of it suggest. That it was born out of blood. The possibility that we are already living in hell. The possibility that humans are hell themselves, and that they committed genocide of an order we haven't conceived to be here. To not live in hell. Cause if all humans die right now, except one, where does morality go? When humans die, God dies with them. When humans die, all of a sudden you are free from judgment from the divine. The bible flamed and the priest unmoving. Instincts. No more cameras when humans die, no more surveillance, no more passport, or driver's license, no more in-world identity. No more property. No more names. No more labels. Would you be able to see the world for what it is? Or would you hide yourself in the thoughts of those that came before you? Would the world gain in colour or go to black and white?

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: The Truth of Isolation and Human Deception
Philosophy.content: Just feel the possibility of that happening, with humans going away except for you, my dearest, maybe paradise comes back. Maybe earth is happy again. Maybe species start filling the sky and land, doing their species sounds, in what becomes the biggest win in a long time. And how much would you wish that others could be there with you witnessing this harmony of things. But they can't. They wouldn't see it. You would think you were crazy. Others couldn't know the truth. You were now so distant, so authentically convinced. And what if you saw this and could go back and tell people about it? Tough truth to digest. And it might be better digested alone. They would throw up, unable to swallow. It would be of the strongest spices they ever let in. Oh my overhuman, are you alone! Stuck in time. You are at the wrong place wrong time, and because of that they don't understand that. In a way that's what they mean when in movies that kind of power can only be obtained by the young mind. For an old mind would fever itself into death, overheating of desperation when confronted with the truth. And perhaps Nietzsche is ashamed that mankind has let their men into such deception, making them die for a lie. And right before dying, did they seize their condition? Did they seize that which was promised? Then again, maybe they convinced themselves they did, for otherwise seeing the reality of things would be soul-fading, as in heartbreaking. Perhaps humanity is lost at this step, when it lies to humans in such a manner. When they discover there was paradise right before their eyes. Maybe that's how humanity gets away with it. If they only realize it when they die, they are not here to advise others. Maybe death was programmed by humanity. Imagine that. Just like in the movie The Island, they get chosen to go to the island. Now it's easy to see now how those of the past went to the island; what is more worth it is to see how you could go, and avoid it. Some went for religion for example.

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: Repeating History and Reading the Future
Philosophy.content: His explanation of inspiration comes very close to converging to mine. Or I perceived this idea and it came to fruition after reading the passage. Nietzsche, I'm here, don't feel so lonely now. And in writing this I realize we glorify those of the past, as in we breed from the past. Maybe no wonder we make the same mistakes as them, no wonder that we learn from history that we don't learn from history, that those who don't know history are doomed to repeat it. But let's not forget ourselves. Those who only know history are also doomed to repeat it. History may be there to shape, but not to make history now. If I can somehow read those of the future, know the future so that I'm not condemned to build it the way it is...

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: Eternal Recurrence and the Will to Live
Philosophy.content: In being immortal one will die several times, he proposes. So is eternal recurrence immortality? Is it to ask that a life is meaningful for you to ask if being immortal with it wouldn't cause you a death of will to live? What if we allow ourselves to the idea of eternal recurrence as the only way to stay living? That this life you choose to live, and in the others you are, a la Sisyphus, condemned to relive it. But not passively. You would have to intend living, for not living it the way it was would be the reason of your death. They say death gives meaning to life, but what if it is the ability of a life to escape death, in that it doesn't need death to be meaningful, that makes it meaningful. And could one succumb to eternal recurrence? I think it would be very very hard, for you are playing against infinity. Victory would never happen, defeat at any time. It seems reasonable to say there would be people at life #3000, life #10 googols, life at any number, yet no one at infinity. Interesting would it be to see how many people would just live life once, in that they would rather live free for the whole of their existence. Or live under the illusion of freedom, memory being erased. How many would sign up for eternal recurrence if they had no choice but to relive their life forever? I mean in a sense that's slavery. Is there any meaningful difference between this eternal recurrence and our lives right now? Are they not topologically equivalent? Maybe time is the biggest difference. Victory is prolonged, unwarning, defeat is immediate and defining. And the sheer curiosity building up as to what lies beyond the recurrence would be enough for people to give it up. If will to live is finite, but unlimited, people die with so much left, so much unexplored territory. The perfect finish would have to die as will to live is redlining. But this is very relative.

Philosophy.title: Human, All Too Human: The Overhuman, Creation, and Smelling the Lie
Philosophy.content: "I walk among human beings as among fragments of the future. The future that I envisage." "Thus I willed it." This, were it not written by this man already, would have been a good lemma for my conquer. As in veni vidi vici. "What would there be to create if gods existed?" Power on us, we will the world, we can create. And if we don't create, others will will for us our creation into existence, and our existence will de-will itself out of it. Did we will ourselves into existence? My words are lightning bolts, and you've been thunderstruck. When you are so good that you are now only competing with those that don't exist, with the myths separated by the barrier of time. "My genius is in my nostrils." "Smell lie as lie." "Why am I a destiny." That in a sense is a question you must seek, a self-evident axiom of your life were you to believe it. The yes-saying with the no-doing I refuse. That in trying to push the herd of sheep to comply to morality we are sacrificing truth, sacrificing change.

Philosophy.title: Ecce Homo: Books as Mirrors and the Simulated World
Philosophy.content: If there ever was a thing that had drawn itself it would be your mind and the world and what they make of each other. Hence they are but one, as in Buddha. To read Nietzsche how he wants, you have to hate him. You cannot read more in a book than that which you already know. Forever in control, you become God almighty, you create yourself and equivalently (implication flowing both ways), the world. Math is discovered in the world but invented in your mind. The world is then not real, just a simulation a la Baudrillard to dissimulate that it is the mind that's real. Not just books, the world itself: because you see it through your mind, you cannot find there more than what you have within. The world is just there so you build yourself. You can run from it, hide from it, but destiny will arrive the same, and your destiny is here, or should I say, it's you. People have this weird way of being domesticated pets of which they are their own owners.

Philosophy.title: Ecce Homo: Zeroth Principles and the Inner Visionary
Philosophy.content: Therefore zeros are not to be found in books, even tautologically. You can start there, but ultimately, you must face the mirror. Zeroth principles came from within, they don't leave a trace. Perhaps if you use the system it will know it was you. In that sort of fashion, to escape it, don't let it know, be a visionary from inside out; when it talks to you it doesn't know it is dealing with a Kant. There is a certain mystique to Shakespeare's "have more than you show, say less than you know". Might explain why one gets the feeling that there lacks authenticity and originality today.

Philosophy.title: Ecce Homo: Amor Fati and the Blank Paper of Reality
Philosophy.content: Amor fati, remorse, blatant anticipation, Nietzsche says, is pointless. Run with what is, for that's what you got. The book is just there so you can read and see your mind. The world isn't there when you are by yourself—not just when you are alone, but when you are by yourself. It was made from you for you to you. Read for your own benefit; history there is already enough, historians there are enough. Right here right now, my page was full, I pondered if that means the reason I had a "creative block" for some minutes. I took off a blank one and voilà, reality realized itself, same as I reshaped it, same as my mind reshaped itself. For him to write give him a blank paper. For him to speak feed him silence, for him to think feed him boredom. Give yourself a blank paper and you shall write. Give yourself the opportunity to make the world come to existence, make the world happen.

Philosophy.title: Ecce Homo: Walking, Caeiro, and the Unity of Being
Philosophy.content: When Caeiro says he knows the truth, it's in the duality of reality that he finds himself. A reality where your perception is enhanced, your soul open, like when you grasp the world and every one of the cells of your fingertips, you feel yourself in and as the world, as one plain sentence, a melody of reality and an harmony of being, where perception stops being and just is. You forget time, the hardest of necessities. Nietzsche suggests that one will never think truly and thoroughly when he is sitting. In walking there is a unification of body, mind, and the world.

Philosophy.title: Ecce Homo: The World as a Reflexive Matrix
Philosophy.content: People are afraid of the world, it's abusive from within. It's that individualistic Nietzsche style brute honesty reflective to oneself. Sometimes people go to the world to find themselves; the matrix that they created may have hints about what it is that people should search for in themselves. Like dreams, that some claim give you access to your mind, what happens in the world may be a hint for your journey, may inspire the reason. It's a reflexive projective world, that in which you live in, because you live in it. To pretend they moved their asses. I am glad I was able to experience this moment, to be given the opportunity of being a human.

Philosophy.title: The Perfect Crime: Murder of Reality and Extermination of Illusion
Philosophy.content: O assassínio da realidade e o extermínio da ilusão: Man this guy Baudrillard really escaped the matrix he helped shine light onto. From the inside the cage isn't visible. The perfect crime in that everything is taken away yet without leaving any trace. It would be the perfect crime if, for example, we now were living in a full blown simulation made by us. That the last semblance of the real world was seen somewhere in 2017 or whatever, that reality extinguished around that area. Like the dead internet theory. Or creating a world for ourselves but then erasing our memories of the original world. What makes these crimes so perfect is that they are so close to being true yet so far. The closest to them is there not being a crime at all. It's a discontinuity. An infinitesimal to the other side and you enter the territory that there is no crime, you see the theory of the crime as conspiracy and hallucination. Says that the crime is never perfect cause the world betrays itself by having evidences of its own inexistence. Even in human endeavors, we feel the need to say something, thereby making the crime imperfect, thereby existing. How can we see that the perfect exists if its existence means that it can't be seen? We can only aspire to see the imperfect existing. The unawareness of the committed crime makes it perfect. One ought to look at evidences for existence of this world as much as evidences for the opposite. Simulated characters in games throw various evidences that their world exists, but what are the evidences that it doesn't? It is hard to escape inside out. It requires some perspective shift to really believe that some occurrence proves your world is not real. Mario seeing Snake being played in his world. How can he guess the concept of a video game to understand that he is in one too? Inside by Playdead. There is an exploit in his world that allows him to be free from the user that controls him. That a clue of the illusion of reality is how submissive it is to us. We mold it, we make hypotheses to which it obeys. Evidências que a realidade apenas está mas não é. That reality has no personality, no character. To rescue reality, to see the leftovers of imperfection of the crime, we have to subtract everything to itself, navigate in the place and not in the map. Not just that the world disappeared, but that the question can't be put, says Baudrillard, that is the death of reality. When no fluctuation no matter how improbable can change the order of things, reality has long died. That things cannot aspire to present themselves in any other way except illusions of themselves. Now Baudrillard in this section seems to me to point out that the world died even before it started, in that he is saying that the real backbone world has disappeared in the world we created by being humans, by having eyes. Sort of the artificial death after the natural death of the world. Both are near perfect crimes.

Philosophy.title: The Origin of Species: Artificial Selection and Human Nature
Philosophy.content: Nós humanos temos a habilidade de definir a nossa natureza, pelo que temos a habilidade de adaptação à vontade. Temos por isso uma seleção artificial em dois sentidos. Podemos alterar a nossa "força" num ambiente. E escolhemos pessoas por aspeto, cor... Se/quando houver manipulação genética, é uma seleção artificial de um modo artificial. É mais um nível de círculos concêntricos de seleções artificiais. Teremos na sociedade um emaranhado de seleções artificiais.

Philosophy.title: Ecce Homo: Autopilot and the Purity of the Zone
Philosophy.content: Nietzschean: When in autopilot, in the zone, enjoy the living hell out of it. That's in some sense the fakest thing and yet the closest to reality you get. So yes, a priori reasoning is finally complete, and it is complete. He who he is (Ecce homo), Nietzsche's purity.

Philosophy.title: Simulacra and Simulation: Domesticated Animals and the Necessity of Pain
Philosophy.content: That animals being industrially bred, without space, is very much the path that humans take in industrial society. Jesus, the similarities between this and the Unabomber are not coincidental. That there emerges a dimension for the mental equilibrium of the animals, their purpose is given back to them in some form. Interestingly, giving humans back this purity of life is either like Kaczynski going back, or perhaps creating not a simulacrum (which already permeates what there is) but an illusion, like the matrix in the Matrix movie. The ingenuity and innocence of humans not knowing that they are being industrially fed into the machines. An hallucination, an imaginary, a real to hide the real. This relates to "pain is the realest feeling you can get". Like Joker says: see their moral, their code, it's a bad joke, dropped at the first sign of trouble. When the chips are down, these civilized people, they'll eat each other. When there's pain, all the imaginaries suddenly become irrelevant. When you are about to die, you are free from the simulacra. There's this inner cannibal inevitable part that flips the switch. That's why torture is done. All of a sudden one changes himself in the presence of pain. Rules don't matter anymore. Artificialities cease to exist, that's the most natural state a human can be in. Sport is a simulation of this, fighting is a simulation, sometimes, of this instinct. When people make time to win by decision, that's a simulacra, with reference to no reality whatsoever. He defends that domesticating animals is even worse than killing them, hunting them and eating them, it's worthy of a greater disdain. Interesting, cause domesticating is in this sense taking out their fundamental liberty, sterilizing them of their meaning, only giving them technology in the form of ration, incubating them in a simulation of their real lives; they are not even worth our respect, they are there for entertainment, for charity. We humans are likewise domesticated by the system, not even worth punishment, just little experimentation rats, lab rats, strategic pawns in the advancement of technology. Quite radical, and one can obviously say that dogs are happy with their owners, but yea it's the opposing view being developed to its logical consequence. Honestly, I'm going to say it right now, 6 February 2024, in the future, if humans go down the route that some of these authors describe, they will be disgusted at how bad we treated domesticated animals. This thing is a Zero, in Bryan Johnson's way of saying, it's so blatantly obvious and it's everywhere, and yet no-one sees it, or the collective hasn't. People haven't yet figured out that meaning transcends the usual longevity, technology-driven quality of life definition. Baudrillard says that this madness is justified in our minds by the hypothesis of the unconscious. And the fact that animals don't speak is what dooms them. Baudrillard follows his own philosophy. He is one of the realest writers I have found. No bullshit, just telling you his way, you may only understand 15 per cent, but you know it's not hyperreal. Very much like Bruce Lee. The most truthful books have the least amount of information, but huge upside in changing your life, or how you see things.

Philosophy.title: The Perfect Crime: The Elusive Truth and The Robbery of the Real
Philosophy.content: This refers to the way I'm reading Baudrillard; perhaps it is my merit that it's my way, maybe Baudrillard can do this to everyone. Man, this Baudrillard remains elusive. I am humbled when I confront his ideas, and so I should be. He presents me a kind of challenge, because of its nature, distinct from the other writers. I must dig to find the truth. His way of thinking obliges me to feel the strain of change. A Baudrillard reader must read his own thoughts if he stands a chance at producing some of his own. This distinction between your thoughts and the thoughts you come up with is often overlooked. You have all the merit or disgrace. The reading differentiates itself because it isn't reactionary, as virtually all are, but actionary. It also demands the ever-fading silence from your mind. A sharp quiet proves necessary for your intentions. It is the exact opposite of a Spinoza. This "perfect crime" suggests to me a consequence of simulacra and simulation. The robbery of the real, perfect crime for its substitution is perfect, more real than the real, the hyperreal, thus going unnoticed. Esvaziando a realidade, colapsando-a nas suas dimensões.

Philosophy.title: The Perfect Crime: Preface, Speed, and the Loss of the Unknown
Philosophy.content: That speed brings locations closer, predictability brings times closer, the will for realism brings reality and hyperreality closer. Times are becoming further apart, through the exponential evolution, or sigmoid. The distance between places is bound to keep on decreasing, until there is hardly a place in the whole of this planet that takes the work once done to reach the Everest for the first time. Unknown journeys will decay, until all is simply connected. This happens until we venture out of this planet, or out of this body of land of ours. Deep space and deep sea still will provide terrain for discovery for some time. Diversity in our species is evolutionary. Previously there were mountains that separated peoples for centuries, and like a company with multiple teams, those people, with no way of communicating, would find their own way of solving the problems of the valley. We are bringing us closer thus bringing us alike. There was no Google or medium from which to see how others solved a given problem. I wonder if AI isolation will make some never leave their bubble and others make their own bubble. Those that like to follow will follow the AI and lose themselves to its will. The others will get up to see themselves as all they have. Hyperreality gives continuity to the world, makes us a single beat. "Só se pode legar aquilo que não se rentabiliza inteiramente". If you aimed for the perfect finish there is no legacy left for the world. That privilege lies only in those who finished before they were finished. Only needs living after death what had yet to live. Perhaps being and living the complete life is about leaving nothing for after. Exhausting your life. As we live longer and longer we increase the standard for what this means. God takes the best of you through your unwavering unwillingness to do the end yourself. What a disrespect towards your life to not end it yourself. To keep on raising the bet, more and more, until it bursts, and you lose everything.

Philosophy.title: Simulacra and Simulation: The Cancer of Collectivism and the Lost Real
Philosophy.content: "Porno, more interesting in the metaphysical sense than in the sexual sense." Maybe he is referring to humans unconsciously building a bigger system than they can grasp, slaves to that system that they themselves created. Very Silo like. Play the game, don't let the game play you. Maybe what he is saying is that the system has gained a life of its own, and we feed him, we make it grow, we stuck ourselves in it. Maybe we are the greatest challenge to ourselves, cause instead of making us progress, we fool ourselves thinking that we are progressing, when it's our creation that has been feeding off of our ignorance. In this way we live in a simulation, the simulation has already been created, and we were its creators, and we are its victims. Hard to not be part of the system; free soloing in this world doesn't seem to take you far. Kinda radical but yes, when we were living real life hunting in the caves perhaps all that there is today is arbitrary, it could've gone so many different ways. It's that dance between being in the system, so that one isn't totally forgotten, but escaping the system to do something new. That cinema is recreating what itself created, is pretending to show the same history that it's destroying, cause now in the mind for the people the history becomes the movie. It's resurrecting a ghost. And remakes are resurrecting a ghost of itself. The reality of movies in the world being shadowed by their simulation in cinema. Holocaust is one of the most simulated things. TV makes the holocaust have a life of its own, without dimension, and makes everyone think they know, when they don't know, they scratched the surface. Creates a distorted view of history, in the attempt of making it realistic. Hyperreal. Is memory a simulation according to Baudrillard?

Philosophy.title: Simulacra and Simulation: Hypercommodity, Information Overload, and the Clone
Philosophy.content: Hypermarkets and hypercommodity. Objects of consumption are now tests, no longer a commodity, to see if you align with the code, they ask us the questions. A simulacra of a thin culture, temporary, is herein created. One loses himself in the social mask. Ads look at you, "you look at yourself in it, mixed with the others, it is the activity of consumption, a game of splitting in two and doubling that CLOSES THE WORLD ON ITSELF". Baudrillard says that we live in a world of more and more information, and less and less meaning, Kaczynski style. He thinks it's because information creates a hyperreality of communication; you think you are more connected with social media, but it's a proxy, a simulacra, it doesn't have substance. Even if you overload yourself with tons of information, there's no depth. Depth in your actions is important, not just genetic programmatic efficiency. An implosion of message in the medium which itself implodes on the real. Medium thus destroys itself, there is a fusion of all these terms. Circularity of vectors, one thing points to itself, points at nothing essentially. Has lost direction by its very nature. "A society purely transparent to itself." A genius metaphor that Baudrillard scratches the surface: says that cloning is none other than metastasis, a cancer of equal cells, ill at their core. Mass collectivism is this, the orientation, the matrix, is nothing but cancer. Cause the cells aren't perfect, at least yet, and probably never. They have mistakes, propagate and become the end of the organism. Says that some people dream of holograms, clones of themselves, and that when it happens it will no longer be a dream, thus losing its charm. Fucking amazing: "when an object is exactly like another, it is no longer exactly like the other, it is a bit more exact."

Philosophy.title: The Origin of Species: The Difficulty of Evolution and Energy States
Philosophy.content: A evolução não é fácil. Porque a tendência é gastar a menor energia e realizar o menor trabalho. Há a inércia filogenética/evolutiva que faz-nos querer ficar no lowest energy state. E a tendência é a taxa de evolução diminuir progressivamente (porque quanto mais energy tem o state em que se está, mais confortáveis nos sentimos em ficar lá). Somos "bons" e não sentimos tanta necessidade de melhorar, e ir de 99% da população para 99.9% é muito mais difícil que ir de 50 para 50.9%. Isto também ocorre porque maximizar a "produtividade" a partir de certo ponto é difícil. Na evolução cada espécie ocupa o seu nicho, explora diferentes recursos de diferentes maneiras. "Struggle for existence". Sexual selection results from a contest between rivals of the same sex. Mudam-se os tempos, mudam-se as vontades - Camões. Os mais adaptados perpetuam a sua natureza, têm uma impressão digital mais definida, até que ninguém consegue adaptar-se ao ambiente, até que a melhor forma de estar no ambiente é de um modo discreto, como a impressão digital por detrás das luvas. (Contribuem, têm o seu nicho, são feitos de matéria orgânica) e a reprodução é manter a chama da vida, da impressão que impingimos no mundo. Essa poderá ser, em última análise, a chama que impele as ações da vida. "A mente é um caos de prazer" - Darwin.

Philosophy.title: Simulacra and Simulation: The Hyperreal and the Murderous Science
Philosophy.content: Baudrillard sets off by saying that the hyperreal, the simulation, has substituted the real. The map is more real than the territory. The representation becomes the reality. "The desert of the real itself." He says that something got lost in the way, the charm of the map. I think he is saying the difference is so little it lost its flavor. Reducibility has taken its toll. The approximation of reality to a programmatic, objective and wrong model, kills the imaginary component, kills points of view. The hyperreal, a pure operational tool. While dissimulation keeps the reality aspect intact, simulating makes distinction between true, false, real, and imaginary arbitrary. Says that images dissimulate the fact that there's nothing behind them, just like in SILO. When simulation happens, everything is dead and resurrected, it has no profundity, it all snaps at the slightest movement, it's a thin sheet, fragile to the careful observer, a dead space only alive at its surface. Science, he says, is in this sense murderous. Says that Disneyland is portrayed as imaginary to pretend that the other things are real. "This world wants to be childish in order to make us believe that the adults are elsewhere, in the 'real' world."

Philosophy.title: Simulacra and Simulation: Historical Duplication, Hallucination, and the Matrix
Philosophy.content: Baudrillard talks about this duplication, say in historical events, which were substituted by their representations as movies, and those camera angles, and those characters, and reality TV, a simulation, as if the cameras weren't there. Much of that idea that this pear that I now eat is the realest thing there is, that the pain Tony Ferguson says is the most real feeling, the rest you can hallucinate. He also considers the abstractions of today's world as simulations which is true, again it's our collective belief that creates this thin sheet, hanging by a thread. Today the entire world hangs by a thread, money, corporations, all are built on top of each other, one small perturbation and the building collapses. We have been piling a deck of cards faster and faster, hoping it doesn't collapse. One could say the 2008 crisis was a mini collapse. In this sense animals live much more a real life than us, without imagination. And yet it seems that among animals we are the ones that escaped the matrix, that escaped our environment. Now what Baudrillard hints to me is that this simulation in our world is much more haunting than what I here described, that's what I want to understand, what is the message. The issue may be losing the sense of truth, that may be the worst consequence. "TV renders true". Perhaps we are ever more hallucinated, with no conscience.

Philosophy.title: The Uninhabitable Earth: The Arrow of History and the Illusion of Constant Progress
Philosophy.content: Há a possibilidade, defende o autor, de isto da ecologia levar ao conflito, porque logicamente, se é uma coisa em que é preciso uma cooperação mundial, não dá para os países quererem ficar à frente uns dos outros. A China tem um rácio culpa/afetação muito maior que a Índia. Até historicamente, na revolução agrícola começou a haver mais conflitos. O autor também fala da história, de que olhamos para a história sempre como progresso. E isto tem de ser assim, não dá para saber que no passado sabíamos menos, porque teríamos de nos lembrar do passado e do que sabíamos no passado. O autor diz que com isto do clima pode acontecer que vejamos um grande regresso, o que discordo. No passado também houve pandemias gigantes, e não pensamos que aí voltamos atrás. "Whatever doesn't kill you makes you stronger". O autor já tinha dito que as pessoas "back in the day" achavam que não conseguiam controlar as coisas. Mas acho que isto é uma ilusão de quem vive no presente e vê o passado. Ao saber que existimos, quer dizer que correu bem automaticamente, e por isso vemos as coisas do passado como acidentes que nos fortaleceram. O futuro no presente é indeterminado. Se pensarmos que somos imunes ao não-progresso, então o risco é ainda maior.

Philosophy.title: The Uninhabitable Earth: Desumanists and the Anthropic Principle
Philosophy.content: O autor procede a falar de um grupo de pessoas, que se podem chamar desumanistas, que acham que o facto de não nos lembrarmos de que as histórias que criamos são histórias nos vai lixar; acham que temos de acordar para a realidade. Mesmo assim poderia ser argumentado que se acreditássemos nisso precisaríamos de cooperação, e a história em que acreditaríamos é a de que as histórias em que estávamos a acreditar eram meros mitos. O autor chega a referir o princípio antrópico, e diz que "a ciência só pode ganhar perdendo". Discordo, porque é errado dizer que a ciência prevê a nossa extinção. Népia. A ciência inclui as outras possibilidades.

Philosophy.title: O Alienista: Dogmatismo, Autoridade e o Viés Científico
Philosophy.content: Um tema que sobressai na leitura é o poder e a autoridade. A verdade é que Simão sabe tanto, mas tem tão pouca sabedoria; está tão convicto, e ao mesmo tempo inconsciente do que está a fazer, o que o torna perigoso, porque com grande poder vem grande responsabilidade. As pessoas resistem a pô-lo em causa porque o veem como uma autoridade. Quando ele vai defender a criação da Casa Verde, fá-lo com tanta eloquência e certeza que as pessoas pensam que ele tem de saber o que está a fazer. Ele sabe que tudo sabe, esse é o primeiro grande erro. Machado critica ferozmente o dogmatismo e o cientifismo. Simão respeita mais os textos de medicina, a sua Torá, do que as suas vivências. O alienista nunca assume que está errado; pelo contrário, está certo e os outros estão a ser avaliados. Bacamarte está para todos os efeitos cego à realidade exterior, porque tem a grade científica sempre na frente. De pensar tanto, encontra imperfeições onde elas não estão. Nos comportamentos vê sintomas, nas manias vícios, na imaginação alucinação. Quase que assume a loucura de todos, incubindo o efeito placebo nos pacientes, enquanto enriquece e fica cada vez mais confiante no seu sistema. Faz da ciência o fim e da vida o meio, ao invés do contrário.

Philosophy.title: The Meaning of It All: The Value of Truth and Interconnectedness
Philosophy.content: "A verdade, a reles paga do cientista, descoberta pelos seus penosos esforços." "Para qualquer coisa que olhemos, se olharmos suficientemente perto, estamos envolvidos com o Universo inteiro." Há mais do que se pode conhecer do que o que se pode provar. Há uma parte muito maior da verdade que podemos conhecer do que a que podemos provar.

Philosophy.title: The Meaning of It All: The Disease of Intellect and Beauty in Science
Philosophy.content: Os cientistas da Idade Média. Eles próprios também fizeram um pouco de ciência, sem dúvida, tal como escreveram comentários sobre os grandes homens que viveram antes deles. E escreveram comentários sobre os comentários. Descreveram o que cada um escreveu sobre os outros. E continuam a escrever sobre estes comentários. "Escrever comentários é uma espécie de doença do intelecto." É importante ter novas ideias. Feynman diz que os poetas reclamam dos cientistas por estes dizerem que as estrelas são meras bolas gasosas. Mas, na verdade, os cientistas é que apreciam a beleza, porque mesmo como meras bolas gasosas eles acham bonito, enquanto que os poetas têm de fazer metáforas e personificações para encontrar a beleza.

Philosophy.title: The Uninhabitable Earth: Climate Change, Adaptation, and Natural Extinction
Philosophy.content: Diria que às vezes não nos podemos esquecer que nem toda a mudança é má, e convém relembrar que não sabemos como será o mundo com um aumento das temperaturas, não sabemos a sensibilidade do clima, não temos noção das nuances e complexidades que o clima tem. Às vezes uma coisa pode parecer má mas na verdade é neutra. O que aqui se faz é definir a sobrevivência dos humanos como "bom" e o que contrarie isso "mau". Se provocarmos a extinção de uma espécie, por exemplo abelhas, pomos em desequilíbrio o ecossistema global. Mas devemos ter em mente que o ecossistema global está mais ou menos sempre em desequilíbrio, porque o equilíbrio ideal é instável; qualquer pequeno desvio tira o planeta desse ponto, provocando movimento no sentido de lá voltar. A alteração da migração dos bacalhaus, por exemplo, não parece ser assim tão significativa e indicativa de que estamos a estragar o planeta, apenas indica que algo está a mudar. O planeta é um sistema que se adapta, e os biomas têm-se alterado ao longo do tempo, e ninguém pensou que era mau ou que havia culpa em haver extinções em massa. Se houver uma extinção em massa por parte dos humanos, não nos esqueçamos que continua a ser uma extinção natural, e talvez aliens mais inteligentes que nós, vendo do exterior, achassem natural, e não mau propriamente, se isso acontecesse. O natural é que mais cedo ou mais tarde nos extingamos, ainda que não queiramos isso.

Philosophy.title: Sapiens: Second-Order Chaotic Systems and the Power of Capitalism
Philosophy.content: History is a second-order chaotic system, in that predictions will affect the outcome of history. It would require a view from another dimension, from outside, to even have a chance at steering the future. In retrospect, every system is in reality an infinite-order chaotic system, in that causal effects are propagated back and forth at the speed of light; they reverberate throughout the Universe, the world affects itself which affects itself. So just approximately are systems first-order chaotic. History teaches us that we will have to make choices, that there isn't anything in the world (and so exists) that will tell us what we will do. We are inside the world. Empty maps were a breakthrough in discovering the world, admittance of ignorance, which goes side by side with the Copernican principle, made the scientific revolution and imperialism possible. It stuns me how much of a simple conceptual breakthrough it was: credit, loans, trust in the future, capitalism, that growth would fulfill itself. It's impressive how limited we are because we impose it. How constrained to move humans were, how much lies beyond the horizon, if just we have the bravery to make certain assumptions, or rather throw off our assumptions. We thought as if we were limited by our ignorance and not by the world. We went from a zero-sum game, as in prey-predator dynamics and harmonic oscillators, to an exponential growth, flipped the sign of the differential equation. I want to live like this, in a capitalistic way, as in exponentially getting tighter and better, before the bubble bursts. Not just in terms of money, but knowledge and so on, productivity especially.

Philosophy.title: Homo Deus: The Paradox of Knowledge and Shifting Directions
Philosophy.content: O paradoxo do conhecimento, como Harari expressa, em que quanto mais sabemos mais imprevisíveis e indomáveis as coisas ficam. A meu ver, sabemos mais, deveríamos prever o que acontece melhor, mas nós próprios somos parte do sistema, e quanto mais sabemos, mais sabemos que não sabemos, e mais potencial então temos para fazer muita coisa e com ela muita bosta. Refere o marxismo, em que as previsões de Marx falharam precisamente porque ele quis mostrar que estavam certas. Ao escrever o livro que comprova que o mundo ia naquela direção, mudou a direção. Há este poder: ao escrever a história do capitalismo está a fazer com que as pessoas comecem a pensar em alternativas. Num certo sentido, ao escrevermos, por exemplo, um livro de 1000 páginas que se chame "Religião", estamos meio que a dizer que tudo sobre religião está ali, ou seja, esta é passada, e agora procuram-se alternativas. Eu explico isto, ou interpreto, como a contingência que Harari não chega a referir.

Philosophy.title: Homo Deus: Intersubjective Meaning and the Pact of Modernity
Philosophy.content: Harari fala do viés para acreditarmos que o nosso Deus e coisas assim são objetivos, enquanto que os da antiguidade não são, porque nos dão um sentido de vida. Como Searle provavelmente diria, podemos ter um sentido objetivo vindo de uma crença subjetiva. "Os Sapiens dominam o mundo porque conseguiram criar uma teia intersubjetiva de sentido". Isto permite a cooperação em larga escala. I would say an intersubjective map of meaning isn't sufficient, it has to be a specific one. If the map of meaning created had properties of pure individualistic competition, then everyone might have competed for the one spot in heaven. As we see it now, in Egypt, believing in the BS of the gods, in the sense that it was a creation, was actually very helpful. We only say this because we don't see that our creations of now are of the same nature. "As empresas, o dinheiro e as nações só existem na nossa imaginação. Inventamo-las para que nos servissem. Então porque é que, quando nos damos conta, estamos a sacrificar as nossas vidas ao seu serviço?" O pacto da modernidade é preferir o poder ao sentido. Estamos a aumentar o nosso poder cada vez mais, perdendo sentido no caminho, e, diz Harari, tentamos com o poder forçar a entrada de sentido.

Philosophy.title: Sapiens: The Experiencing Self, the Narrating Self, and Fiction
Philosophy.content: Harari diz que nós não somos um "indivíduo", mas sim um "divíduo", e que temos dois operadores: o ser da vivência e o ser narrador. Relaciona isto com aquela experiência da dor do Kahneman, em que o narrador distorce a história para que ela se possa repetir. Por vezes o da vivência toma controlo e desfaz o que o narrador fez. "Yet none of these things exist outside the stories that people invent and tell one another. There are no gods in the universe, no nations, no money, no human rights, no laws, and no justice outside the common imagination of human beings." Now I remember that I had this "discussion" as a child, as many may have had. I distinctly remember asking my father if a company is its CEO. Else what is it? If said company pays taxes, someone has to pay them, right? So is the company the person who makes the payment? "Telling effective stories is not easy" - hence it being hard to be a known author, and contaminating the meme pool, leaving a noticeable footprint.

Philosophy.title: Sapiens: The Necessity of True Believers and Cognitive Dissonance
Philosophy.content: And now it strikes me that some people think that climate change doesn't exist, just like some think that COVID doesn't exist. This is the collective belief in storytelling. They don't exist, we invented them. But the point is for everybody to believe in climate change, or COVID, so that we can (we think) stop bad things from happening. And these people can make the conspiracy that "oh, the government wants us to believe this narrative," which is true, but it's not the government or this or that, it's the nature of man, at least according to Harari. Just like God, human rights and so on are all fictions of the imagination, even though useful, or actually just for being useful have they become things. "There is no god, but don't tell that to my servant, lest he will kill me at night" - Voltaire. And it's freeing in some sense, it's some sort of truth to know this, and according to Harari there have to be some true believers, maybe because the structure of the fiction, like a building that was built, would be too fragile otherwise. Harari brings forth the idea that cognitive dissonance is a feature, not a bug, in that for cultural evolution and for us to do what we do, essentially we hold some contradictory ideas. No one has to figure out all the necessary truths and make sense of the puzzle of the Universe; no one has to cover every hole in our reasoning to do something. We can have so many complexly related beliefs and act according to them. It makes us more prone to action, and as Harari says, dynamism and creativity.

Philosophy.title: Sapiens: The Arrow of History and the Principle of Unity
Philosophy.content: He points to an arrow of history, in which civilization tends to unity, despite bumps along the way, like the collapse of world-encompassing empires. Evolution makes a lot of new species, but eventually, it may become more likely that one starts to take over the market, the space, and dominates the landscape. Unity in this sense would be tending, in terms of living beings, towards the optimal fit for survival. Also, tending towards unity could just be the same as stating the infinite betting theorem. Which is to say, that eventually what is more likely is that something will have enough "power" to take over and bring the other stuff to zero, from where a stable equilibrium occurs. Like betting against an infinite machine, eventually, a long enough string of losses takes you to zero for definite. This unity principle can be seen through those games where you have a map and several circles like Agar.io or AI wars, that start small, and then eventually you are going to end up with one that dominated the rest. Or if you think of colors, a mix of all the colors is a higher entropy state, so the 2nd law of thermodynamics can also relate to this unity thing.

Philosophy.title: Causation in Science: Constraints, Determinism, and Fatalism
Philosophy.content: Most theories out there, as noted by the author, are only constraints that normally define the direction from cause to effect but fail to actually identify what is the cause for a given effect. This notion of causal constraints is indeed a true one. The way I see it, determinism and stability are two very different things, and their overlap leads to the whole separation between the two types of symmetry. He goes on to clarify the concepts of determinism, stability, necessity, contingency, and fatalism. He reinforces the power of thinking of contingency as a fuel for critique, because we blame it on ourselves rather than necessity. I should say that fatalism isn't properly wrong. Entropy, for example, is approximately fatalistic, and a lot of philosophical statements are (for example, the skeptic saying you can never be omniscient, or figure out the entire Universe because you are part of it). The author says that the relation between events and laws is of the same character as that between laws and symmetry. Symmetry, rather than being a consequence or coincidence of laws, is a constraint to which they should obey, given that without an invariance we don't discover stuff.

Philosophy.title: Alan Watts: Causality and the Fallacy of Correlation
Philosophy.content: About causality: "laws of nature are regularities observed". But you can see something regular because you use um meio regular: usas sempre a mesma régua. Se tu adquirisses outra perspetiva terias outra lei. Se tu não adquirires nenhuma perspetiva então chegas à verdade. Esta falácia da correlação acontece, por exemplo, entre dinheiro e wealth. Making a law is walking backwards, but you don't REALLY get to the beginning, therefore you just see correlation and you make an approximation.

Philosophy.title: Sapiens: Language, Gossip, and the Collective Imagination
Philosophy.content: Pensa-se que é a linguagem que levou à nossa ascensão sobre os outros animais. Um aspeto é que baseamo-nos em palavras, em vez de frases feitas, o que permite muitas combinações. Ou seja, pensamos na forma de variáveis, o que nos deixa fazer infinitamente mais com quase o mesmo espaço de memória, ou eficiência. A fofoca é mesmo uma teoria sobre o sucesso da nossa língua, porque permite cooperação: sabemos e conhecemos melhor uns aos outros quando fofocamos. Talvez, na minha opinião, por termos em conta não só a relação que temos com uma pessoa, mas a relação que essa pessoa tem com as outras. Para a cooperação em larga escala, serve a ficção, acreditarmos em mitos comuns. Acreditamos em algo que se sobrepõe a todas as pessoas, de maneira que não precisamos de conhecê-las todas para trabalhar com elas. Somos uma equipa inconsciente, porque temos uma imaginação coletiva. Por isso somos ilimitados.

Philosophy.title: John Searle: Language, Internal Monologue, and Collective Intentionality
Philosophy.content: "O locutor deve ter a intenção de que o ouvinte reconheça que o locutor teve a intenção de que o ouvinte reconhecesse que o locutor teve a intenção de significado do locutor." And it is only now that I realize how unbelievable language is. I realize that I am talking to myself, having ideas talking to myself. How did humans without language think? They didn't talk to themselves. But it is true though that ideas don't need words. The feeling of the idea is what makes me remember it. Searle fala dos princípios pelos quais a linguagem se rege, e um deles é a discretitude (as palavras são descontínuas). Intuitivamente, este princípio sugere uma artificialidade na linguagem, uma organização tipicamente humana. "Um dos segredos para fazer boa filosofia é permitir-se ficar espantado com aquilo que qualquer pessoa sensata toma como garantidamente óbvio." Searle discute o problema da intencionalidade coletiva. Uma coisa que distingue muito bem os humanos dos outros animais é que atribuímos funções de estatuto a humanos. A minha intuição diz-me que alguém concebe um mundo possível onde acha que as coisas funcionariam melhor e, se houver aceitação dos outros da ideia, sobre ter alguém com responsabilidade por fazer algo (especialização), então pode ser implementada. O poder, segundo Searle, é satisfazer as condições de satisfação nas coisas em que a direção de ajuste é da mente para o mundo. Or in other words, having things a certain way because you want them a certain way.

Philosophy.title: John Searle: The Conscious Unconscious and Freud
Philosophy.content: "No meu caso, vivo no mundo das aparências, e isso é quanto baste para mim." Searle diz que o modelo de Freud de termos estados inconscientes que depois "a pessoa entra e lança luz sobre eles" está errado porque isso próprio é a consciência. Mas para mim faz sentido: you unconsciously get conscious, and unconsciously get unconscious, when you go to sleep and wake up for example. Which leads to the question: when waking up (unconsciously getting conscious), are you conscious or unconscious? Searle's answer would probably be conscious, but mine is unconscious.

Philosophy.title: Consciousness A Very Short Introduction: Descartes and the Root of the Problem
Philosophy.content: O autor diz que se se conseguir dizer a função importante da consciência, de maneira a que tenha sido incorporada nos seres vivos, basicamente teríamos uma solução do problema. I'm getting the idea that maybe the root of the problem (that we have no solution for the problem of consciousness) is when it was created, by Descartes. In saying "I think therefore I am", Descartes finds that there is a difference between the thinking and the material, namely that his thinking is necessary, but the rest of the world isn't. This happens because the way to eliminate thinking would require thinking. By the way, the way to prove thinking, as Descartes did, also requires thinking and experience, meaning that we get into a circle. So technically no one ever proved that we are conscious, that is not necessary. What is necessary is that we can neither prove that we are not conscious nor that we are conscious. This conundrum happens because thinking is both the medium through which we see things, and also the subject of those things. The thing is, first-hand experience tells us that we are conscious.

Philosophy.title: John Searle: Intentionality, Direction of Fit, and Speech Acts
Philosophy.content: I realize now that Searle uses that species of objectively subjective kind of thing. "A consciência, embora seja a fonte de toda a relatividade ao observador, não é em si relativa ao observador." Ao falar da intencionalidade, começa a fazer conexões entre linguagem e mente. Distingue a direção de ajuste: da linguagem para o mundo e do mundo para a linguagem (e da mente para o mundo e vice-versa). Diz que há atos de fala em que os dois sentidos estão presentes ("onde se muda a realidade representando-a como algo que foi mudado") e afirma que estes atos de fala são a chave para entender a civilização humana. Isto adequa-se aos positive feedback loops, em que dizer que o mundo é de uma determinada forma repetidamente leva-nos a acreditar que é dessa forma, e muitas vezes o mundo muda mesmo ajustando-se ao que descrevemos. O papel do storytelling. Searle fala que a intencionalidade é a representação das condições de satisfação, que vêm das crenças, desejos, arrependimentos. Parece-me bonito dizer que nesta rede de intencionalidade o estado mental é uma combinação ponderada de cada um dos desejos e crenças.

Philosophy.title: John Searle: Representationalism vs Direct Perception
Philosophy.content: Searle começa a delinear aquilo que diz ser um dos maiores desastres da filosofia ocidental: dizer que o que vemos é mera representação do mundo real, que vemos um objeto do mundo real como quem vê uma alucinação. Mas isto é um erro, diz ele. A alucinação não é ver algo. A alucinação é ver nada, por definição. É uma causa interna que leva a mente a ter intencionalidade para o nada. Depois nota que dizer que uma impressão a que temos acesso é semelhante a algo no mundo real não faz sentido, porque não podemos comparar algo visível com algo invisível para atestar que são iguais. The thesis of Searle shrugs off some problems, but in the process, I realize that it makes a lot of sense. This thesis is just an acceptance that if this isn't the real world that we are seeing, then we can't know stuff. Let's bet that this is the real world, and go with it.

Philosophy.title: John Searle: Intention in Action and Derivative Intentions
Philosophy.content: Searle começa a falar das intenções prévias, intenção na ação, e as mediações causais e constitutivas que se englobam num evento complexo. Um exemplo intuitivíssimo é a diferença entre levantar um braço e dar um sinal. No contraexemplo de Bennett, em que a pessoa pensa que não matou a outra mas depois esta acaba por morrer, Searle diz que não foi o seu tiro que causou a morte, mas sim um efeito colateral, e por isso a ação não é intencional. Isto tem um pequeno problema, porque normalmente queremos que haja transitividade na causalidade. A forma simplíssima de resolver isto seria definir a intenção como "querer que o estado do Universo seja de tal e tal forma". A única forma de não haver intervalo entre a ação e o alvo da intenção é a ação ser a intenção. Na palestra 6, a pergunta é o que se tem de acrescentar aos animais pré-linguísticos para que tenham uma intenção do tipo humano. A principal característica que assinalaria é a imaginação. Searle diz que a intencionalidade consciente é prévia à intencionalidade da linguagem. A diferença entre os humanos e os pré-linguísticos é que os humanos estão mais avançados nas ramificações e mediações das intencionalidades, o que lhes dá maior liberdade e potencial. "Só somos bem-sucedidos ao fazer o mundo ser como queremos se a nossa tentativa de fazê-lo ser desse modo for a causa de o mundo ser desse modo."

Philosophy.title: John Searle: Reconciling Realities and Zeno's Paradox
Philosophy.content: Searle propõe "apresentar várias perspetivas acerca de diferentes assuntos que constituem partes de uma única resposta a uma única pergunta". Ele vai falar de consciência, livre arbítrio, linguagem, ontologia, objetivismo e subjetivismo. Tal como ele, pretendo unificar consciência, causalidade, livre arbítrio, caos, aproximações e infinitos, absoluto e relativo. Searle diz logo que a tradição é fazer com que sintamos que vivemos em várias realidades. Como exemplo, vai buscar a suposta irredutibilidade da consciência que é usada para criar a separação entre o mental e o físico. Daqui aflora-me rapidamente a intuição de que there is no such thing that can be called a cause, nor is there such a thing that can be called an effect, only a formula. Isto é, shit just happens. No paradoxo de Zenão parece mesmo haver novamente esta coisa do "it just happens". Parece que apesar de haver uma assíntota, passamos por ela. Passamos o infinito. Se tivermos um gráfico com uma assíntota e estivermos com um movimento através das abcissas, por incrível que pareça, passamos para a outra parte do gráfico, depois da assíntota. Embora estejamos infinitamente a chegar ao infinito, digamos em x=5, acabamos por passar para o 5.000001. É impressionante que passemos uma infinidade de pontos no espaço até chegarmos ao caracol num tempo finito. But it just happens. "O meu objetivo não é, sublinhe-se, reconciliar ciência com senso comum. Essa pareceria uma missão disparatada: os resultados científicos, uma vez aceites, são senso comum." Great point, that there is no need to reconcile science with common sense because common sense isn't spontaneous, out of nowhere. It is built, and only after that can you interpret stuff.

Philosophy.title: John Searle: Realism, Materialism vs Dualism, and Fractals
Philosophy.content: Searle diz que o Realismo não é uma teoria, mas sim uma condição prévia para haverem teorias. Determinism in some sense is also that. You bet that there are all the conditions for you to know everything, because you want to know as much as possible. Diz que o seu projeto é mostrar que a humanidade é uma consequência natural do mundo de partículas explicado pela ciência. Mas mostrar que a humanidade é uma consequência natural da física é apenas uma forma de mostrar que são ambas partes da natureza. A verdade é que entre duas partes da natureza há sempre um gap, não interessa quão pequeno. For that not to be the case, either something is different from itself, or there is nothing to compare (the whole). Now when it comes to infinity, things get weird, cause in a fractal for example, I can show you a part and you have no idea where it is on the fractal, cause there are infinite parts like that, and it's scale-independent. You approximately always have a scale, though. For you to be observing it, you have to be something with scale, unless you yourself and the rest of the world were a fractal. The thing is, when you look at that part, you are looking at the whole. It's always the whole, because infinity plus 1 is still infinity. Searle now killed it. He says something that I hadn't realized. Materialism makes the same mistake as dualism, because both reject consciousness as an entity of "normal nature". The materialist, by asserting that consciousness is reduced to atoms, for example, may be saying indirectly that consciousness itself isn't a thing. Both approaches take us away from the "it just happens" principle.

Philosophy.title: John Searle: The Principle of Identity and Strong AI
Philosophy.content: Searle dá uma definição geral de consciência e enumera características que parecem poder ser explicadas a partir das contingências. Uma delas é que a consciência funciona causalmente, que causa o movimento do braço (o pensamento faz o mundo material mexer). A consciência nota que o braço mexeu, mas quem diz que a consciência está por detrás disso? Searle afirma que há algo no cérebro que causa consciência, mas que, através do seu Princípio da Identidade, quando levanto um braço, não são 2 eventos (1 neurobiológico e 1 consciente), mas sim 1 evento com 2 descrições. This last principle of identity comes back to "shit just happens", and if generalized, you would get a universal identity, meaning that the Universe is one event, although you can analyze it in many different ways. The issue Searle finds is that not using the principle of identity leads you to go find how and why the brain causes the consciousness. Searle refuta a IA Forte. Sendo que ele defende que a consciência é algo causado por algo no cérebro, não é possível que um computador execute um algoritmo que o leve a criar uma máquina consciente? Se a consciência tem a sua essência em algo no cérebro, não podia a engenharia desenvolver um hardware com essa estrutura? O problema do Quarto Chinês: a forma mais fácil seria perguntar algo em chinês que o algoritmo não pudesse responder, ou fazer perguntas estúpidas a que o computador seguiria regras fixas, enquanto que o humano diria: "O que significa isso?". O computador interpreta o chinês como um conjunto de tarefas, não "sabe" chinês. Talvez o computador só adquira a semântica quando ele próprio a definir tendo contacto com os seus propósitos.

Philosophy.title: Chance and Necessity: Indeterminism and the Illusion of Alternatives
Philosophy.content: Monod says that it is obviously impossible to conduct an experiment which proves the non-existence of purpose in nature. Monod diz que um dos objetivos do livro é mostrar "that the biosphere does not contain a predictable class of objects or of events, but constitutes a particular occurrence, indeed compatible with the first principles, but not deductible from them and therefore essentially unpredictable." Isto é, há um indeterminismo em que havia muitas opções que poderiam ter acontecido. This type of indeterminism sort of leads us to P vs NP, since there are multiple possible living beings that we can verify are compatible with the laws, but it's way harder to actually find from the laws which is the actual one. I hope I'm wrong if I say that Monod fell for the trap of the intuition that living beings could have been otherwise (just change something along the way, like the asteroid not falling, and we would have seen a whole different set). Evolution compels us to think it is legitimate to think this way, since the dynamic comes with that many more factors that would have altered the course of events. The thing is, nobody changed anything like that, we are in this version of the world, and here, if no non-deterministic laws are to be accounted for, there only could have been these animals, for example. There is quantum, though, an example of something that could bring about that probabilistic factor or possibility factor that Monod invokes.

Philosophy.title: Chance and Necessity: DNA, Chance, and Pure Creation
Philosophy.content: Monod introduces the principle of randomness: that initially these things are random and that is what gives them the potential to develop into virtually anything (protein folding increasing entropy, etc). Monod states that DNA is the fundamental piece of invariance. It's the constancy across the living world (albeit aliens would probably have other forms of replicators). It's the source of continuity between all living beings, and a great one. That said, it's the source of evolution, because change is only significant against something more unchanging. "It necessarily follows that chance alone is the only source of innovation in living beings. Pure chance, absolutely free but blind, at the very root of the stupendous edifice of evolution." "Absolute coincidences, those, that is to say, that result from the intersection of two totally independent chains of events." Those in my view are only an approximation. First, there are phylogenetic trees; then there is causation at the speed of light. If they are close enough in space-time to intersect, they are also close enough to exert a causal interference on each other. For Monod, although the building of molecular proteins is a revelation rather than creation, evolution is pure creation for its own sake, necessarily pure chance. Others believe that evolution is the uncovering of nature's yet uncovered design. I see these two as perpendicular vectors, and Monod chooses the absolute: goes with the perpendicular vector and rejects any space in between.

Philosophy.title: Chance and Necessity: The "Just Happens" Principle and Objectivity
Philosophy.content: Monod actually refers to the "just happens" principle. He states that the probability of there being a certain type of organism before the universe, or before planet Earth, is infinitesimal. "Yet the universe exists, particular events must take place in it." He understands that to break the approximation you have to predict when it happens. "Destiny is written concurrently with the event, not prior to it." About the principle of objectivity, Monod says that ethics and knowledge aren't separate, and to go for knowledge is to have in the first place the ethics of knowledge. Monod says that values and knowledge are necessarily embedded in action and discourse. I agree, since you can only have approximately 0 knowledge involved in a statement or action, and the same with values. These separations that we make are artificial lines. When they are not there, we just have a space of possibility, in which there is a line, for example centered in the origin of the multidimensional space. That is the line we identify in our world, and what we do to try to explain it is to divide this "vector" into components along axes that we sometimes forget we ourselves created. At the end of the day, Monod is saying that it is necessarily the case that in biology you have to account for necessity (strict limits according to physics), and yet pure chance makes evolution unpredictable in such a way that you can't derive it solely from the laws of physics.

Philosophy.title: Against Method: Religion, Progress, and Falsifiability as a Measure
Philosophy.content: The thing about God and with it, religion, is that you ain't gonna progress because you already got the answer to everything. How are you going beyond something that you already don't understand? Outra coisa que separa Deus dos átomos é que os átomos são uma entidade possível. Deus não pode existir num possível mundo, porque senão já não é Deus. You don't ask: does God exist in this possible world? You ask: does God exist? Sobre a incomensurabilidade: maybe the measure of comparison which lets there be approximately a commensurability is falsifiability. According to Popper, scientific theories are made from this principle, but maybe not only that; how scientific they are is a measure of their falsifiability.

Philosophy.title: Chance and Necessity: Reproductive Invariance and Teleonomy
Philosophy.content: A invariância reprodutiva é a informação, o conteúdo que deve ser passado à geração seguinte. A teleonomia é o projeto geral da espécie, em que tudo o que contribui para esse projeto, normalmente a reprodução, é teleonómico. O nível de teleonomia de uma espécie é a quantidade de informação que tem de ser transferida para assegurar que o projeto se realiza. Daqui pode vir aquela conversa de que os humanos escapam à teleonomia por serem conscientes e terem os seus próprios projetos que, na sua perceção, são maiores do que o projeto teleonómico. Uma pergunta interessante é se um projeto que valoriza a reprodução tem necessariamente de criar todas as coisas que a favorecem. Com os humanos, não é a reprodução à bruta que domina, mas sim o investimento no longo prazo.

Philosophy.title: Chance and Necessity: Natural Selection and the "Mass of Existence"
Philosophy.content: A cena é que nos esquecemos que não é a reprodução o "ultimate project". A seleção natural é simplesmente um postulado estatístico probabilístico, tipo o aumento de entropia. Sendo uma subespécie, por exemplo, uma fração de todos os seres vivos num local, quanto maior for essa fração relativamente às outras subespécies (até que nestas seja 0), mais temos a tendência para dizer que a outra subespécie originou esta. Embora o que vou dizer agora nos desvie para um domínio subjetivo, na verdade, para dizermos que uma espécie evoluiu para outra, não é propriamente o número de indivíduos que interessa, é a visibilidade da espécie no meio das outras. Pode haver seres vivos que nós nunca vimos que têm o maior número de indivíduos, como uma bactéria, e outras bactérias de que temos vestígio transformaram-se nela. Sendo o tal argumento estatístico, é a "massa de existência" da espécie que conta. Claro que quanto mais reprodução houver, mais massa de existência, mas também digo que quanto mais tempo viver um indivíduo de uma espécie, maior a massa de existência. The point is, from natural selection to saying that reproduction is the goal, we have to be aware that there was a leap, even if a small one. And so we should rethink it. Natural selection may only be a good approximation for evolution. There can be fluctuations in and out of natural selection due to its probabilistic nature. It's more likely for a species to be recognized as evolved from another if it exists significantly. Also, we've got to factor in that natural selection is "after existing". Living beings before being born are not tied up to living according to natural selection. Natural selection comes after they are born; it is a consequence of the conditions that we are given.

Philosophy.title: Against Method: Creating Concepts and the Art of Invented Language
