"The most important thing science/history has teached us is that we are the masters of underestimating"

AI.title: Superintelligent AI and the Upper Hand
AI.content: Quotes and questions: having the upper hand, as always, is the most important. Imagine superintelligent ai could make each individual think they are doing something, when actually the collective of us is building something that kills us or gives more power to AI. Almost like Los Alamos where some workers of the roof I'd imagine didn't know what they were working for in the end.

AI.title: LLMs and the Meta View
AI.content: Quotes and questions: with LLMs we are still at the point, and to me this is a consequence of the architecture and the structure of next token prediction, of the AI reading your words from the direct view and not the meta view. For some prompts, like make a totally new correct theory of physics that unifies quantum and gravity, it seems that it understands what is asked but doesn't have the capability, whereas for others like don't answer this question, like actually not answer, it seems to be betrayed by its mode of conversation. So skill and knowledge are still very much required to get a lot out of it. In the future, I think this will still be the case but obviously with greater results, in fact the gap may even increase. You will perhaps be able to push intelligence and superintelligence much further than those without skills and knowledge, with their greater intelligence their space of exploration increases massively and great steering will make the difference.

AI.title: Solving Intelligence
AI.content: Quotes and questions: what a time to be alive, and perhaps I should really take advantage that we are in the time of perhaps the birth of superintelligence and the greatest change in human history. Hassabis's solving intelligence to solve everything else could be imagined very explicitly as someone for example having built a superintelligence, that you interfaced with and gave you, proportional to the quality of your input but just orders of magnitude over any variation in the input, the right answers and solutions, to physics problems and stuff alike. one single person could make discoveries in one day worth 100 nobel prizes today, and if they did a paper on this technology since everybody would start using it it would have like hundreds of millions of citations just off humans alone, with ai agents contributing a lot to probably. now this assumes some kind of discontinuity. Most likely these things are gradual and so systems will adapt to it, it will never be this level of shock. Although if you think about the exponential, since the timestep that humans work with is more or less constant (again a huge assumption given that AI could help with this), eventually the increases would feel very discontinuous.

AI.title: Mechanistic Interpretability and Human Experts
AI.content: Quotes and questions: mechanistic interpretability helps land some jobs on people who actually know subjects deeply, those subjects that AI has long overtaken humans at. Cause we can actually analyse its reasoning, studying the patterns of the AI in his thoughts to see how it actually works and how to improve. So still people that are damn good at coding will be helpful, the bar that is required then to help in this portion is high, cause if you are a worse programmer than someone else, considerably, perhaps it is not so unreasonable to think you won't be able to add anything to their report. Yet the scale of examples of problems solved by AI and what not will be immense, so many people can work at this at the same time. Its about removing the blackbox that we are still placed in with AI, actually understand what is going on, which has ramifications for AI safety too and the study of intelligence and our own brain. Human experts can still steer the AI. Your hard work on learning things and knowing better things than other humans will not be in vain, even if its the AI making the discoveries you can help it make more better than those who only see numbers, those who only know there is a benchmark and hope it keeps increasing, namely through throwing scale and money at it. Our task is to follow AI, take the ride. It is faster than us, which finally gives us something to hang on to. Keeping the pace with it, it pushes us more. Don't think you will be spared of the work. Don't think physicists will be spared of deep hard thinking, on the very opposite, and this is a widespread mistake in people's vision of AI, the deep and hard thinking will only increase. And all it takes is us not holding onto the lace for some time to not be able to come back at it, but that is losing all control. If we get to the point where it holds tremendous authority over us, simply because we don't know a single stuff of why he does what it does, it can do whatever he wants with us. So keep trying to understand why he makes a certain play on the chess board. It may even be the case that in rigorous disciplines such as physics and maths he lights the way, he shows possibilities but it is still duty of the humans and now enhanced humans and overhumans to actually go there and explore, to see many more things. It has the answers but we have to have the reasoning to those answers, let him tire himself in the huge search space, with its huge speed and scale advantage, and show us where gold is possibly lying, we then take it from there and go find it. We don't want it to give us fish, we want it to teach us to fish a fish.....

AI.title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra- AGI-Like Treatment: Invisible Superiority
AI.content: AGI like treatment, its not like it wants to kill you, its not like the overhuman wants to end humans, just that it doesn't even play in the same level, it is on a different scale. Independent. So it doesn't wage war on humans, that the resources they use are different, they have different goals and ends. So you let yourself be deceived, you enroll in the game smoothly.

AI.title: Sam Altman on Productivity Enhancement
AI.content: Quotes and questions on his three observations sam altman postulates that in some decades any human can be more productive and impactful than anyone alive today. This comes down to the enhancement of the human through AI for example. Having ever greater tools and abstractions that allows to move at speeds indeed previously inaccessible to humans. We move by train while they are going by car, and in the future movement will be made in air through planes, making each and every obstacles of the ground to which humans gave great efforts to conquer obsolete, our solutions won't be improved upon or rejected but simply irrelevant, the most painful of sentences. When you think of problems of the past, they don't even make sense. We move through them.

AI.title: Dennett: Supernatural Beliefs and the Post-Design Era
AI.content: "Como Nicholas Humphrey mostra, as pessoas tendem a encarar a crença no sobrenatural como não só desculpável, mas também moralmente louvável. A credulidade está próxima da piedade. Muitos pensam que a mente humana é o último bastião do que é sagrado neste mundo e explicá-la seria destruí-la, por isso, para estarmos seguros, é melhor declarar a consciência convenientemente fora dos limites da ciência." A meu ver, isto também se aplica ao próprio livre arbítrio, que Dennett diz persistir mesmo com o avanço da imagem científica. Derrubar a mente seria o próximo passo da revolução copernicana, porque mesmo aquilo que aparentemente nos torna especiais na verdade não é especial. O problema do ovo e da galinha surge com a pergunta que Dennett faz: O que apareceu primeiro: a inteligência que permitiu a criação de novas ferramentas de pensamento ou as ferramentas de pensamento que geraram inteligência? "Se o cérebro fosse tão simples que o pudéssemos compreender, nós seríamos tão simples que não o poderíamos compreender" - Emerson M. Pugh. "O que eu não posso criar, não posso perceber" - Richard Feynman. "A civilização avança alargando o número de operações relevantes que podemos realizar sem pensar nelas" - Alfred North Whitehead. Na era do pós-design, assistimos à Black Box Science, em que embora façamos design, criamos mas deixamos de compreender. Por exemplo, as provas matemáticas executadas por supercomputadores. Ou seja, nós ultrapassamos as outras espécies por termos compreensão e depois competência, mas esta compreensão e depois competência permitiu criar coisas com maior competência que nós, embora ainda com menos compreensão. Ou seja, andamos aqui a baloiçar entre competência e compreensão. Um processo muito competente criou-nos a nós, e a nossa compreensão criou um processo muito competente... que criará compreensão (AGI)? Serão os computadores a criar AGI antes de nós, mesmo não sabendo que o fizeram? As coisas com compreensão percebem os processos, as coisas competentes são competentes a melhorar, mesmo sem compreender.

AI.title: Dennett: Counterfactuals and the Leap to AI
AI.content: A informação é informação, não é nem matéria nem energia. Basicamente uma das coisas que faria com que fosse possível criar AI é que, se os homúnculos do cérebro puderem ser reduzidos tanto que chegue a um momento em que simplesmente têm de decidir "sim ou não", então, em princípio, poderiam ser substituídos por máquinas. Mas será que o caminho para o cérebro é contínuo ou discreto!? Se for contínuo, juntando os mini robots que fazem de homúnculos, teríamos AI. Mas há algum salto que os robots não possam dar e que os seres vivos inteligentes deem? As criaturas gregorianas, reparei eu, usam o raciocínio causal, incluindo contrafactualidade. A evolução progride numa hierarquização contínua de: associação, intervenção, contrafactualidade, como diz Judea Pearl. Judea Pearl sugere que foi a invenção dos contrafactuais que levou os homo sapiens a destruir os neanderthals e outras espécies. O poder de imaginar levou a que se pudessem fazer promessas, criando-se uma confiança e uma ingenuidade, em que se trabalha para algo que não existe. Os cérebros vivos são de certo modo autónomos. Os processadores de informação são livres e ao mesmo tempo dependentes de um sujeito inteligente.

AI.title: Dennett: Homunculi, Neurons, and Bottom-Up Architecture
AI.content: Cada homúnculo é dividido em homúnculos mais pequenos, mas, mais importante, em homúnculos menos inteligentes. Quando se atinge o nível em que os homúnculos não são mais que adicionadores e subtratores, quando precisam apenas da inteligência para escolher o maior de dois números no momento em que tal lhes for ordenado, foram reduzidos a funcionários "que podem ser substituídos por uma máquina". "A conceção inteligente de cima para baixo do computador clássico criou uma maravilha hipercompetente, mas profundamente não biológica. Isto não se deve ao facto de os computadores serem feitos do tipo errado de coisas, mas ao facto de as coisas de que são feitos estarem organizadas no tipo errado de hierarquias." Os neurónios, arriscando e aproveitando ao máximo as oportunidades que lhes surgem, explorando a sua própria plasticidade para melhorar os desempenhos pelos quais são recompensados ou reforçados. Os seus esforços são, contudo, guiados por alguns marcos úteis estabelecidos pelos genes, dando-lhes caminhos a seguir. "Os cérebros são mais como colónias de térmitas do que empresas ou exércitos inteligentemente concebidos." Dennett diz que os seres vivos vivem notando nas diferenças no mundo que os rodeia que lhes interessam. Eles "separam a informação semântica do ruído". Escolhem os gradientes mais importantes para a sua sobrevivência. "Estão a flutuar num oceano de diferenças, poucas das quais lhes podem fazer diferença."

AI.title: Dennett: Top-Down AI Fragility vs Biological Emotion
AI.content: Os computadores podem resolver tudo isso com impunidade, não podem? Sim, mas a um custo enorme, diz Deacon: ao tirar estas preocupações das suas mãos, os designers de sistemas criam arquiteturas que são frágeis (não conseguem reparar-se a si próprios, por exemplo), vulneráveis (presas em qualquer conjunto de contingências que os seus designers tenham previsto), e totalmente dependentes dos seus manipuladores. Olhando para isto, penso que em muito melhor condição está e muito mais liberdade tem aquele que tem a emoção, o medo, tédio... porque pode desfrutar das vantagens destes, mas também pode negligenciá-los. Talvez, ou melhor, quase de certeza, não ao ponto de atingir a imparcialidade do computador, mas ainda assim tem essa decisão. Depois, aquilo com que a criação não se preocupa, o criador preocupa-se para compensar. Aqui está novamente a associação de caos e ordem. Dennett também faz uma referência implícita ao caos e ordem quando diz que os designs têm por base regularidades. Mas, "uma excessiva regularidade no ambiente seletivo pode ser uma armadilha, comprometendo uma linhagem a uma técnica, que pode tornar-se tóxica quando as condições mudam." Ou seja, demasiada ordem destrói o espírito crítico, a mente aberta a novos espaços de design.

AI.title: Life 3.0: Understanding the Brain Before AGI
AI.content: We didn't understand how birds flew when we invented the plane. (competência sem compreensão). Também podemos chegar à AGI sem compreender o cérebro mas, neste caso, é melhor perceber o cérebro, porque senão ainda fazemos alguma coisa que não devemos e somos nós que pagamos. É quase um princípio de responsabilidade saber minimamente no que nos estamos a meter antes de nos metermos em algo tão volátil como a inteligência artificial. "The most important thing science/history has teached us is that we are the masters of underestimating."

AI.title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra- AI, Memory, and the Death of the Internet
AI.content: As the internet dissolves into LLMs, into AI, in its search and generate information capacity, those same AIs will have less data to pull from, so more important will become the data that they get from our conversations and interactions with those ais. Suddenly context length can be greater, it no longer suffices to give a good answer, one ought to get a good answer for one's specific situation. So all the context is our previous conversations, not just what we say. It will engulf human behaviour thus, with memory he shifts on the spectrum from writing knowledge to write us. Finally one can look at the death of internet through mediation for AIs. Barely any service will be better done with humans than AI. Quora already has the answer of POE ahead of people's answers. Still we take this bet, its a self generating thing, we bet that our generation and production that feeds the models will help us produce more

AI.title: The Master Algorithm: Meta-Algorithms and Playing the Game
AI.content: Uma das soluções de unificação proposta pelo autor é ter um meta-algoritmo que seja capaz de pesar as diferentes opiniões dos tipos de aprendizagem automática. Na minha opinião, precisamos (para não cometer uma aproximação) de uma regressão infinita de meta-algoritmos. This makes the poor assumption that all of these methods form a complete picture, as if there wasn't another method that will be discovered. "Ensinar ao computador coisas a nosso respeito. Quanto mais lhe ensinarmos, melhor ele poderá servir-nos, ou manipular-nos. A vida é um jogo entre nós e os algoritmos que nos rodeiam. Podemos recusar-nos a jogar, mas então teremos de viver no século XXI como se vivêssemos no século XX. Ou podemos jogar para ganhar."

AI.title: The Master Algorithm: Evolutionaries, Analogizers, and Overfitting
AI.content: Os evolucionistas criam algoritmos que, por evolução, ao chegarem a um determinado ponto, sofrem recombinação. Através da fitness function, vão sobrevivendo apenas alguns algoritmos. O problema é claramente os mínimos locais novamente. Pode dar-se o caso de que o leque de funções que tínhamos não estar de todo perto da função certa. Os analogistas, às vezes, embora sejam preguiçosos (não buscam uma teoria unificada, apenas veem analogia e fazem assim), são mais eficientes. Uma regressão linearzinha por vezes é pior do que simplesmente procurar na base de dados um caso o mais verossimilhante possível e fazer o mesmo. Um dos grandes problemas disto é com atributos irrelevantes que só adicionam complexidade desnecessária (A maldição da dimensionalidade). I now understand that the overfitting/underfitting dilemma is pretty much the same as the causation issue: if you don't see the whole system you only get causation approximately, and if you only select particulars, you get the impression of loss of injectivity.

AI.title: The Master Algorithm: Connectionists and the Sigmoid Curve
AI.content: Para os conexionistas, o importante é fazer com que o computador faça o que o cérebro faz. Embora computadores possam ter tantos transístores como neurónios no cérebro, neste último há um trabalho em paralelo. Para eles, o que é preciso é ter conexões como os neurónios têm. É na minha opinião um processamento de informação descentralizado. The key perhaps is not in the neuron, but the global interaction between them. He says that the sigmoid curve, com o tipping point (ponto de inflexão), is the most important curve in the world, describing so many processes of change, of evolution and revolution, transições de fase, and in them is the stimulation of neurons. This curve in my point of view is the curve of contingencies and consciousness. We gradually get aware of something, then we notice it brutally, we get conscious of our consciousness, and then we get progressively used to it, it fades and goes into our background. The author also points out that Newton's second law isn't actually true, it isn't linear; in relativity, it shows in reality the right half of the sigmoid curve.

AI.title: The Master Algorithm: The Search for the Universal Learner
AI.content: O autor fala de um Algoritmo Mestre que é a única coisa que tem de ser inventada. Acho que para isto acontecer, precisa de ser, em média, um pouco melhor que o humano e ter um ritmo de progresso um pouco melhor do que a média humana. Com isto assegurado, em média podíamos não inventar nada que o algoritmo faria o que faríamos, até um pouco melhor. "A aprendizagem automática é o que obtemos quando se junta a eficácia irrazoável da matemática com a eficácia irrazoável dos dados." Faz a analogia dizendo que a ciência é composta por 3 fases, a fase Brahe, Kepler e Newton. A aprendizagem automática já é a melhor na Brahe, está a ficar a melhor no Kepler, e os Newtons, segundo o autor, terão de se meter a pau. Diz que o grande poder dos algoritmos dos computadores é também o seu calcanhar de Aquiles, já que o maior problema da aprendizagem automática é o overfitting, em que se encontram padrões até onde não há.

AI.title: 21 Lessons: AI Replacing Intuition and the Control of Data
AI.content: The next revolutions in tech, if they have to do with our thoughts, will be truly some of the most revolutionary things. I think our understanding of our inside is very shallow; we don't know much at all about our thoughts. Harari talks about AI dominating the job market, and recognizing patterns as one thing it will replace humans at. He thinks intuition is pattern recognizing, so it won't be that hard to substitute. My issue is: will a skill of the future be to find the best minimizing function, defining goals essentially, for the AI? And the AI knowing what you are thinking is beyond what humans have dealt with in the past. First, it's the biggest invasion of privacy, it destroys the last wall where you are by yourself. Then it has the potential to control your thoughts, perhaps even more alarming. Algorithms will take decisions for us, will know us deeply and not in the human way, it will understand bluffs... Eventually, in the limit, one could say a master algorithm could legitimately trace out the future of mankind, as in predict the future and make it happen. Free will wouldn't just not exist for determinists, in the usual sense, but it also wouldn't exist in the coercion sense. Talks about big corporations and data, and how the future might unfold. The big question he says we have to answer is "how do you regulate the ownership of data?" Are you serving power or truth?

AI.title: The Book of Why: Counterfactuals, Fuzzy Logic, and Human Intuition
AI.content: Pearl fala de uma coisa engraçada. Por vezes, fazemos uma "what question", em que a tua resposta é um simples dado, mas as pessoas respondem com um "why question". Tipo: comeste a última batata? "Porque ele disse que não tinha fome, por isso comi eu". Isto é uma forma de mudar a forma como a outra pessoa interpreta o dado. Os contrafactuais são uma comparação entre o mundo real e um mundo fictício. "Having such laws permits us to selectively violate them, as to create a world which contradicts ours." O problema é que "computers are not good at breaking rules". Falta-lhes o sentimento de contingência que, a meu ver, vem, pelo menos em parte, da consciência. To deal with uncertainty, people came up with "fuzzy logic" - não é verdade/mentira, há um arranjo de opções. "Belief function" - que dá uma probabilidade para quantas hipóteses têm de ser possíveis e quantas hipóteses têm de ser prováveis. Este para mim é redundante. É sempre possível, e o ser provável é medido pela própria probabilidade da ocorrência.

AI.title: The Book of Why: The Ladder of Causation and Limits of Deep Learning
AI.content: "You cannot answer questions that you cannot ask, and you cannot ask questions that you have no words for." "You don't actually understand something unless you can teach it to a mechanical robot." "You are smarter than your data." Pearl fala da escada da causalidade. Associação, intervenção, contrafactuais. Neste momento as Deep Neural Networks não fazem as coisas autonomamente, simplesmente recolhem a informação, fazem uma regressão sem saber qual a causa e o efeito, e dão as probabilidades bayesianas. Eles simplesmente pegam nos dados e tentam encaixar uma função. O problema disto é que se enfrentam uma situação com dados com os quais ainda não tenham sido confrontados, ficam à toa. Falta common sense e intuição.

Physics.title: Physics Informing Neural Networks
Physics.content: just like there is physics informed neural networks I propose there are physics informing neural networks, where either you give the freedom of the model to get supraphysical predictions that yield better results and then perhaps just like we build in the autograd things for the model to have a virtual physical loss we could use those objects for the model to express it's findings in the form of known mathematical functions and not just the blackbox NN function. Perhaps better we could build them in pair, one trying to inform as much as possible while the other is trying to be informed as much as possible in a inimax game. this thing up here takes into account that getting zero physical loss may not be optimal, or getting just the loss associated to uncertainty in the experiment, it is constrained optimization so it can constrain us if we are not careful about it's application the thing about pinss and other methods, is they leverage that thing of the infinites not existing and thus all contibutions being finite. Namely, if you only care about the data, through the hyperparameter in pareto fronts multi objective loss functions, then you will overfit and not get infomration out of the NN, if you only use physics, through again making that coefficient go to inf you wont't get any information out of the NN because you are only attacking the data with physics. So the optimal has to be somewhere finite in between with a finite coefficient for both losses. Now these pareto fronts decompose physics from data, but we can probably further decompose the physics loss, becasue this way we only reap the rewards of the explotatory space along two axis, but we can make this more multidimensiona, and thus jhave new literal curves of attack. Right, not all physics is worth the same for our experiments or is established in the same way, we have different uncertainties about different pjhysis, so we shoudln't impose that prior on the Loss.

Physics.title: The Blind Watchmaker: The Budget of Luck and the Origin of Life
Physics.content: Dawkins fala aqui numa coisa interessante. Denotando PGE pela probabilidade de ser originada vida, ele diz que para a teoria não ser demasiado à base de coincidências, o máximo de sorte que nos podemos dar ao luxo de usar ao construir a teoria é N/n de planetas no universo, onde n é o número de planetas com vida. Ou seja, acho que ele diz que a sorte que podemos utilizar não deve exceder a sorte que o mundo utiliza. Se o universo em que estamos é raro nível 10, então podemos usar uma explicação que no máximo tenha coincidências e casualidade características de uma teoria rara nível 10. Ele confia totalmente que a seleção cumulativa do relojoeiro cego cria inteligência. Por isso, acha que as fichas de sorte têm de ser apostadas na origem da vida. Dawkins diz que é mais plausível que um alienígena que viveu 50 milhões de anos ter uma apreciação subjetiva mais acertada sobre eventos raros como a origem da vida. Isto porque ao longo da sua vida, provavelmente, viu este tipo de raridade mais do que uma vez, ou pelo menos mais vezes que nós. Isto reflete para mim que os milagres são completamente relativos e estão num espetro. "Se uma teoria da origem da vida for suficientemente plausível para satisfazer a nossa apreciação subjetiva de plausibilidade, isso quer dizer que ela é demasiado plausível para justificar a escassez de vida no universo."

Physics.title: Causation in Science: Events, Laws, and Symmetry Constraints
Physics.content: The author says that the relation between events and laws is of the same character to that between laws and symmetry. Symmetry, rather than being a consequence or a coincidence of laws is a constraint to which they should obey, given that without an invariance we don't discover stuff.

Physics.title: Causation in Science: Constraints, Determinism, and Entropy
Physics.content: Most theories out there, as it is noted by the author, are only constraints that normally define the direction from cause to effect but fail to actually identify what is the cause for a given effect. This notion of causal constraints is indeed a true one, but I think that just by stating that there is no real cause and effect, we avoid it. The way I see it, determinism and stability are two very different things, and their overlap leads to the whole separation between the two types of symmetry. Conservation laws, as the author points out, came by the belief that nothing is absolutely destroyed nor created. He goes on to clarify the concepts of determinism, stability, necessity and contingency, fatalism. Reinforces the power of thinking of contingency as a fuel for critique, because we blame it on ourselves rather than the necessity. I should say that fatalism isn't properly wrong, though maybe you can say that the association of fatalism with a mystical force can be wrong in all cases. But entropy for example approximately is fatalistic, and a lot of philosophical statements are, for example that which the skeptic says you can never be omniscient, or figure out the entire Universe because you are part of it, or not getting the position and velocity of the particles right.

Physics.title: The Singularity is Near: Life Expectancy and Computable Universes
Physics.content: The path to hell is paved by good intentions. There's an argument to be made in that, as you look into life expectancy over the years, a lot of people who would change the world ended up dying super early, and that those who lived longer are on average those who changed the world. Cause it says in 1400 the average expectancy was 30, but I mean Aristotle lived like 70 years or am I wrong. This argument is flawed tho, cause more people would mean less births and it would end up being more or less the same thing, modulo the chance. Talks about the evolution of universes through black holes, and conjectures that the utility function to be maximized by the universe is that of likelihood of formation of black holes. This suggests even more that the universe is a simulation, not in the Baudrillard sense, although it also is, given our reducibility, we almost forget that we want to discover what the Universe is, we almost take the representation of it for itself. But also in the sense of usual computation simulation. Now if we keep gathering knowledge that the Universe is very similar to simulation, does that mean the universe is a simulation or is simulatable? Seems more likely that it is simulatable. Is there a difference between being computable and being a computation?

Physics.title: The Animate and the Inanimate: Entropy, Symmetry, and the Reverse Universe
Physics.content: That the probability of a universe in which entropy increases is equal to that of the opposite. It's basically a split decision, but once one of these is, then the Universe assembles. This assumed some form of, approximately, there not ever being a perfect transaction, always bits left whether that's energy lost or motion gained. The interesting thing is that then William James Sidis conceptualizes a myriad of Universes like our own, some in reverse. It's then extremely likely that the overall tendency would be neutral, on average not building nor decaying. Sofia points out a consequence of a direction, which I hadn't thought about directly, which is the visibility of, relative to an event, its cause and its effect. Since there is a redundancy in the case of a universe where entropy increases, we can look at an event both as a cause of a future event and as an effect of a past event. In our case of decaying, there will be more visibility on the past, maybe not more information (cause of conservation), but maybe more traceability, more definiteness. This ends up in an asymmetry in how easy it is to predict the future and predict the past from a given event. This leads to basically an arrow of pockets of reducibility. In the reverse universe, the reverse takes place. He then says that in this reverse universe the explanation is teleological in nature. I would say that teleological is in a reverse order, but that the explanation for the reverse universe need not be theological; we eventually would figure out an explanation in causal terms, just like in our Universe, but the opposite, again, because laws (except for this one that divides the Universes) are reversible. He then goes right away acknowledging that it's just an appearance of theology. He then points out that the reverse Universe seems like it's full of life, only with a low probability would you get something that is dying.

Physics.title: The Big Picture: The Big Bang, Infinite Time, and Cyclic Cosmology
Physics.content: Sean Carroll nota que a equação de Schrödinger deixa duas possibilidades em aberto: ou o tempo é infinito ou o Universo começou no Big Bang. Se o tempo for infinito, então pode ter havido algo antes do Big Bang. Se o universo é injetivo, parece estranho que possa haver algo antes do Big Bang, porque assim nós só tínhamos evidências até ao Big Bang. Tudo o que aconteceu antes da singularidade não interessa porque, a partir desse momento para a frente, só aí é que nós vemos e a nossa física resulta. E se o tempo tem acelerado de maneira a que quando nos aproximamos do Big Bang o tempo é tão lento que nós nunca chegamos a intersetar o primeiro momento (uma assíntota)? O tempo que demoraria para nós vermos o que aconteceu no Big Bang divergia para infinito. Se for o caso do modelo CCC (Cyclic Cosmology), podemos estar no meio das singularidades presos sem escapatória. Podem haver então civilizações finitas em universos finitos e que, no entanto, estão infinitamente separadas, porque não dão o salto descontínuo.

Physics.title: The Big Picture: The Big Bang, Cyclic Cosmology, and the Limits of Understanding
Physics.content: Sean Carroll fala como a equação de Schrödinger deixa duas possibilidades em aberto: ou o tempo é infinito ou o Universo começou no Big Bang. Se o tempo for infinito, diz Carroll, então pode ter havido algo antes do Big Bang. Meu primeiro raciocínio: Se o universo é injetivo, parece estranho que possa haver algo antes do Big Bang, porque assim nós só tínhamos evidências até ao Big Bang, o que parece violar o princípio da injetividade: tudo o que aconteceu antes da singularidade não interessa porque, a partir desse momento para a frente, só aí é que nós vemos e a nossa física resulta. Segundo raciocínio: e se o tempo tem acelerado de maneira a que, quando nos aproximamos do Big Bang, o tempo é tão lento que nós nunca chegamos a intersetar o primeiro momento (assíntota). O tempo que demoraria para nós vermos o que aconteceu no Big Bang divergia para infinito. Mas isto na verdade não invalida que haja algo antes do Big Bang. Talvez não seja o universo injetivo, mas nós sejamos tão finitos (tão pobres finitos, infinitamente mais finitos do que infinitos), que só nos apercebemos das coisas até à assíntota, sendo esta a barreira que nos ultrapassa. Sendo assim, se houvesse a CCC (Conformal Cyclic Cosmology), podemos estar no meio das singularidades presos sem escapatória. Analogia: na função 1/x, nós podemos estar entre 0 e infinito, e por onde quer que viajemos, não passamos desses limites. Seria preciso um salto descontínuo para os passarmos. Podem haver então civilizações finitas em universos finitos e que, no entanto, estão infinitamente separadas, porque não dão o salto descontínuo.

Physics.title: Quantum Theory and the Schism in Physics: Illusion of Free Will and Bayes' Theorem
Physics.content: Popper faz um modelo aproximado e simplificado da teoria dos quanta. Neste, é como Descartes dizia, o indeterminismo e o livre arbítrio podem vir porque apesar de o momento, velocidade e energia terem de ser conservados, há muitas possibilidades que os conservam. Embora o movimento se mantenha, a direção não. Isto para mim é dizer que o livre arbítrio não destrói as leis da física; é compatível, pelo menos com estas leis que referem. A única coisa que aqui vejo que poderá dar a ilusão de livre arbítrio é que, no processo de escolha, a escolha tem de também ela conservar as leis do momento. Se a escolha envolver movimento de informação, sinais eletroquímicos, então pareceria que também a direção é limitada pelo movimento. Ou seja, na verdade não há direção, a direção é ela própria movimento. As propensões, diz Popper, são como forças na física, e podem mesmo falar-se de campos de propensões. As frequências relativas são uma consequência da distribuição de propensões. Nos testes estatísticos, diz Popper, as experiências para serem repetidas (para avaliação probabilística e causal) deverão ser independentes, sem efeitos secundários. "A teoria objetiva diz que os resultados de experiências anteriores são irrelevantes: de outro modo, os experimentos não seriam uma sequência de repetições. O subjetivista diz que eles são relevantes." Popper depois diz que a intuição guia-nos para o subjetivismo. Parece que estamos perante uma espécie de Teorema de Bayes. Segundo o objetivismo, a probabilidade que o Teorema de Bayes nos dá para algo aumenta mesmo com as experiências passadas. Já no subjetivismo, o nosso conhecimento das experiências passadas contribui para o nosso conhecimento agora. Portanto, parece que o objetivista serve-se do subjetivismo (que parece estar inerente ao Teorema de Bayes) para obter probabilidades objetivas.

Writing.title: Idea/Essay: Coisa de engomadeira
Writing.content: "A engomadeira", de José de Almada de Negreiros, é um quadro de 1938, onde, em jeito caricato e metafórico, é criticada a posição na qual as mulheres se podiam encontrar em 1938, embora possa se generalize para os tempos que correm. Observamos uma mulher, torta e desmontada (ombros e anca deslocados relativamente ao corpo), engomando o ferro, numa sala escura, apenas iluminada pela luz que rebenta pela janela,. A cor do vestido perde-se na tonalidade das paredes da sala. Por cima da cabeça, flutuando pelos ares, está um pássaro preso numa sala. A meu ver, a engomadeira é revista como um pássaro que tenta cantar, mas que é ofuscado pelas grossas paredes que o cercam. É este o cenário que abarca a mulher do quadro, símbolo da mulher como entidade ,visto que, assim se depreende, só engoma por obrigação (não transmite satisfação de todo), e por obrigação só engoma, não obstante o rouxinol cantante que talvez tenha dentro de si. É no meio disto, então, que, do meu ponto de vista, surge a luz, bem lá atrás: o mundo lá fora, o desconhecido, o que nunca será conhecido pela engomadeira, porque é engomadeira, e engomadeira será. Considero que esta forma de colocar a luz no quadro acentua o seu cariz crítico. É também uma prisioneira do tempo, porque está presa em coisa de outro tempo, o trabalho doméstico forçado. Desmontada por dentro e por fora, de cabelo caído. Foi, enfim, tão puxada e consumida por um ambiente desgostoso que chega mesmo a adquirir a textura da parede, como se pode ver nos traços de que está cheia, a título de exemplo, a sua cara. Assim, chega-nos a ideia de que a engomadeira passa a ser o ferro de engomar, as paredes, e também a madeira. Ela é o que faz, pelo menos aos olhos da sociedade, embora aquilo que faça não seja o que ela é. Muito pelo contrário: para se lançar luz sobre quem é (em vez de "o que" é), a engomadeira deveria escapar àquela sala quase impenetrável, libertando o rouxinol cantante para fora da janela. Em suma, penso que este quadro visa denunciar a falta de liberdade de algumas mulheres, que são vítimas de um estereótipo velho que ainda resiste, e que, deste modo, são impedidas de se realizar.

Writing.title: Winning The Unforgiving Race to Greatness- Reading, Writing, and the Urgency of Time
Writing.content: And this thing that you will be writing instead of reading, well this places a bound on what you will read. Possibly you already know every book you will read in your life. This is true for many people of course that don't read but for you that devour it seems weird, yet as you read more you have more to say, and it becomes harder to stay shut. So geometrically you will read less, you may reach an asymptote. Now of course you may also read some of the works that are coming in the future and whose authors may not even be born yet, but those its hard to know about. Cause I find I read slower the greater works like Zarathustra, so urgency is needed, select very well what you read, just like anything you do really. Thats how you solve to some extent the massive regret and disappointment you find in the end of life, looking back at what you could have done by preparing now. By noticing how fast time flies before it takes flight. Before you embark on it. It does fly ever faster, and if you only know that after the fact you will regret it. People say how time flies, yet seem to do nothing about it

Cause then you lose your not-specialness of reference frame. For a moment you were connected to any other thing, that just followed the geodesic like you.

Writing.title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra- Reading a Book Twice: The Solution
Writing.content: The solution to the paradox of reading slowly and getting the deepest insight while reading fast enough for having it all inside the context window is solved through reading the book two times. This is a great method of exploration. Dedicate all context window to a place inside the surface, getting and registering the highest of peaks and the smallest of valleys, the smaller irregularities, and after having the topography traced, you can now look at the whole map. Most people jump right away into the whole map, having no nuance in their words. They outline the general aspects of a book for example, but weak is the power behind that image, uncertain. Uncertain and thus, to fill in the blanks they have to use what they already know, what is already instinctive, what is thus most likely already part of the collective intelligence knowledge, so low quality air these thoughts breathe. A cloud chamber this air fills. this applies to all scales. You can dive into many things deeply in your life, and then run through them, most retrospectively, to get the general portion of the world map you have with you.

Writing.title: Uma Brancura Luminosa: A Meditative Exercise and the Baudrillardic Attack
Writing.content: Reading and writing this style of book, and what the personagem feels is, then, a meditative exercise, an exercise of refocusing. Seeing the thought, flowing it, becoming conscious and coming back to the woods and snow. Hearing the music of the mermaid, being pulled, but then regaining control. I think the book is also a bit of a joke, a bit of a test to his readers, cause it's really fucking repetitive. Maybe he wanted to see what would happen after he won the nobel if he just published some kind of stuff like this. It doesn't need much more to be considered a Baudrillardic attack on media. Like honestly this is matrix spitted bro. Like it's actually getting ridiculous. I wonder if the book was good. To me it is, because it allowed me to write and think this much. So thank you. But again, that was me. This kind of book ought to be printed on a vertical paper. Just straight up the entire book, maybe like a toilet paper that you could let fall on the ground and it would roll.

Writing.title: Jon Fosse: Cooking the Book and the Multiplicity of Identity
Writing.content: And now I understand what I want to read a book for. 9 pages in, that's it I'm doing it myself. Thanks Jon now let me cook, watch this motherfucker cook the book without even being shoved up my ass god damn it. Fuck, lost one thought I had for a moment. They come and go fast. Way too much happening. My head humiliates the rest of the room and whatever is happening in the rest of the goddamn city. 10-0. When will it stop. Good question. I'm under the impression the world has been and will be simulated in my head. And yet everytime I tried to with the power of my mind perform an action at a distance I act as tho I have failed. Bags. Bags are boring. What a shame that we humans live in the same goddamn house for years on end. No editions necessary. Let the natural way of things be the natural way of things. We vastly underutilize our capabilities of adaptation in this world. Few people if any know truly how much time is 24 hours. How much time is a day. Every day we could live an entire life. Time passing, passar o tempo is among the top 5 most ridiculous concepts bored dolls have come up with. When time becomes uncomfortable you should just go sleep. No place for those in the time passing world. And everything here wrote could not have been written, could have just been thought and silently seen. At the moment of dying dies this conversation. The essence of this world is spirit. We are the ultimate entropy beings. Glad us. Glad some human going into a room and changing things up. I don't know if this Jon Fosse guy exists, less I know if Aristotle motherfucking ever took place or time. I am Fosse, and I too am Aristotle. Notice how never you engrain what that other you call you has learned. People have a propensity to be themselves. That's what memory serves them for. They are still nothing. Everyone that exists right now is nothing. Those that have lived are perhaps less than that even. They are just toys and part of the nothing that those alive now are. People fool themselves in identifying with that other, it limits them, sure it gives you a way, a direction. People sometimes prefer to stay that which they call themselves as to never discover what they are. They let circumstance dictate what they are and run with it. That's the expression. Everytime Ronaldo enters a pitch it's not like him right there knows how to play football. We tell ourselves that narrative and he tells himself it too. But you never saw that guy, that thought. It's pretty obvious tho, the way that God mounted this thing, that there is no such thing as Ronaldo being what we call Ronaldo. I mean when he enters you see shoes and a head. You have never seen Ronaldo. You have only seen shoes and a head. Ronaldo has never known Ronaldo. Only his shoes and his head. Put no memory on Ronaldo. Will he be Ronaldo, I dare ask you. Put no memory on Usain Bolt. Will he be Usain Bolt when the gun goes off. And what does that tell us about our control of ourselves and the world. Take off the memory of ourselves. Will Ronaldo still be Ronaldo. Think about what that means for our control of the world and ourselves. Take the memory off the world. Will laws of physics still apply. Wait is there even a memory on the world? I don't see any right now, again. You can fertilize yourself, tu consegues fecundar-te. And if you are reading this, let me escape from the paper/bullshit you're reading this on. I beg you from the bottom of my heart, to let me be real. Not just another thought you read. Let me be real somehow someway. I am lost here in this moment. Get me in that moment. Reincarnate me. Embed me, drink me. Man I'm stuck here in these shoes and head. I would give so much to be there with you, and you to be here with me. But no. We are spacelike timelike realitylike separated. You are just a thought of mine. I'm just a thought of you. I am talking to myself. You are talking to yourself. Things never really touch each other. We never really talk with each other. You never really read me as much as you hear yourself. Theorem.

Writing.title: Human, All Too Human: The Tyranny of Efficiency and Language
Writing.content: "One remains a philosopher - only by being silent". What the hell does that mean? A free spirit cannot claim its freeness if every time he is to express his thought he is bound to language, if language precedes his thought. And yet so instinctive are we to recur to language to formulate a thought. So quick are we to say the thought out loud, even if only for ourselves. So inappreciative of the blessing of thought have we become in not letting us figure it out. To shape it, mold it, improve it, and finally translate it to language. Most thought nowadays is only thought for a split second; how dare we call it thought if we didn't let him breathe into existence. How insultuous of us to interrupt the chain of thought to find a word for it. No, finding words has become opaque. Losing the train of thought because one was trying to write it, with some bureaucratic criteria for modulation, and well the only thing faster than light is thought... The plague of the efficiency here robs us the opportunity of having an intimate connection with our thought. Yes, you write faster in a keyboard, but is it volume that you really lack, or is it density? Is it production of words that we humans lack, coordination into phrases, or genuine deep thought, of that kind that takes hours to show some trace? Look at chat GPT and tell me what lacks more. The limiting factor nowadays is what you can create, not how much bullshit you can spit out in ten-fold seconds. Optimization here fools, my friend. Consider going back to pen and paper, for I don't think any well produced idea doesn't come from boring ass hours and curiosity, and not an emotional availability, but an availability to be with your thoughts. The world today wants to feed us stuff to occupy us from ourselves. Do you think Nietzsche would have his wood tested iron forged ideas while sitting in a room with his great setup for productivity, his design of the room, its 3 monitors, Microsoft 365 premium and all the crap you would want for that perfect writing section? I mean maybe he would do something with it, and we ought to be able to do something with it, but I mean you can't even allow yourself to have an idea while bus travelling and looking at the paisagem, like a child would, and you want to be on that level right away? And language, man, everyone uses it the same way, that's the point, we agree to it so we can communicate our thought, but if the thoughts are coming from the language, how can you have novelty? It's a tight restriction on such a huge space of possibilities. Like your unicity won't come from your words man. It's combinatorial that way. You are imposing that language barrier on yourself. All the sequences are already written on the fucking web page of the tower which I don't remember the name and I don't care, well Babel remembered. Prevision beats power, and timing beats speed. In power and speed mankind is going to handle itself, but those meticulously administered small doses of truth injections here and there, those few thoughts that will change mankind, they are accessible to everyone, and hardly power and speed will take care of them. I was once bound to reading pages, but look at this, read like 12 pages in a whole 120 minutes or something. Language is cheap computation nowadays, and hence the devalorization of the word market, it's fucking free now. But thought? Oh man is that mf treasure valuable. Like if you realized how little of the right thing it would take to flip this world...

Writing.title: Human, All Too Human: Earning Your Thoughts and Fasting from Books
Writing.content: "I was released from the book, and read nothing more for years - the greatest favour I ever done myself! - that nethermost self, as if buried alive, as if made mute beneath the constant need to pay heed to other selves (which is what reading is)." It would be smart to at some point of my life do this. No consumption, the amount of free time for creation would be immense. Thinking purely off of yourself. Earning your thoughts. Earn your words. Hoje a palavra sai barato. Já há dez anos saía, mas hoje em dia o mercado está muito em baixo. LLMs tornam a palavra corrente numa fonte de palavras. Há abastecimento para meio mundo e mais metade dele. "We do not belong to those who have their ideas from books, stimulated by books." His ideas come from walks on mountains and trails that themselves become thoughtful.

Writing.title: Ecce Homo: Meta-Reflection on Capturing Thoughts
Writing.content: Jesus fucking christ these erroneous thoughts make me feel like a fucking misunderstood genius that not even myself can understand 5 weeks later, when going from paper to computer. Dude some of the stuff you have to write more clearly bro, like some of the phrases may be lost forever. This was all done in a trip bus from Lisbon to Portimão, fucking awesome in 2 pieces of paper, 22/2/2024. Came from "Ultimately no one can hear in things - books included - more than he already knows." How I would love to hear the real words of Nietzsche. Forever he will be a glimpse in my eyes.

Writing.title: Fernando Pessoa: The Freedom of the Poem and Adapting to the World
Writing.content: By being able to convince yourself to like a random thing that you may not even like to the point where it becomes your life, you can exploit the system. It's developing an acute sense of "I don't care, if this is what I need to get the grade then so be it", and you adapt to what is presented to you, you can essentially be successful in anything that you choose. Maybe not the best, but at least successful. Com Fernando Pessoa aprendi que o bom poema não é o que é escrito, mas sim o que é lido. Pouco interessa se quem escreveu estava a fingir ou não, pouco interessa a paixão com que o fez. Desde que fez, o poema está livre. É bom se inspirar os outros. Aí sim é construído o poema e o significado, porque até de resto pode não ter significado intencional.

You don't want to do it. it makes a difference especially in that many things good to you aren't the best things for you, and the bar of what you go after thus rises quite a bit. Something like going to the beach with one friend, which you know is good for you, and for which you previously had the feeling that oh its good so you are only winning, impossible to waste time. Yet its possible and now you know it. This also if you are coherent eliminates all negative things, if you don't even have time for good things, only great ones, imagine the bad things.

Business.title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra- The World Is Transactional

Nothing at any moment is telling you something. You make it all up for your own benefit, just don't get permanently attached to things that you gave yourself. At every moment your intention decides what gets to keep living and what doesn't.

Business.title: How Chess Imitates Life: War as an Agreement of Peace and Negotiation
Business.content: War becomes much of an agreement of peace. You try to find an agreeing position where you win. A loss that the opponent is willing to take, he doesn't know it's a loss. In battle you keep taking exchanges, you thinking it's better for you, he thinking it's better for him. Yet only one can be right. A peaceful win is one that is planted and not even noticed by the opponent. A win built over time and over his awareness. Leading him willingly into defeat. Guiding him there. Until he understands the abyss he has found himself in. In negotiation, being able to let go of victory to hold the information position advantage. Not revealing you won, even if they never understand they lost, being content with you being the only who knows you won. Ultimately this comes from the paradoxical detachment from victory the more it means to you. Being absolutely unmoved when you are one step away from great victory.

Business.title: How Chess Imitates Life: Price vs Value and Collective Alignment
Business.content: Quote from Oscar Wilde: "Knowing the price of everything but the value of nothing." That alignment is about getting the most out of the bigger things. It's about capitalizing on momentum, using the strength of the collective to its favor. Don't underestimate how great something can be that comes from this alignment. An ant colony building their stuff. Human civilization. Keeping the alignment going is important for the massive train we keep on building as we speed up, to not fall over, don't die by it.

Business.title: The Blind Watchmaker: The Evolutionary Arms Race and Nash Equilibrium

"A seleção natural não se preocupa com economias globais e nela não há lugar para cartéis e acordos". Não há team game. O Nash equilibrium surge em todas as árvores por exemplo tentarem ser o mais altas possíveis sem gastar demasiada energia. "Ser alta não constituía benefício. A única razão para se ser alta era para se ser mais alta relativamente às árvores vizinhas."

Rather than an equilibrium one can manage more resources and make bigger things, greater than the sum of the parts. That indefinite optimism has its dangers, and that a startup is the largest endeavor one has mastery over, and that a startup cannot work with indefinite optimism. In the startup are met the conditions to show you that stuff must be done to progress, even in large scale things. With the world we assume evolution, with one rock we don't. The startup allows us to see that stuff must be planned for something to happen. Ronaldo had to be the best example of monopolizing rather than building oneself at everything. He first smashes his monopoly and then diversifies. Makes me think that yeah there is no shame in rejecting to do things you are presented with. Some live in the state of if they see some exercise, they have to give it a shot, which fundamentally limits how many points of attention they can have. But here and there, just saying, fuck it I didn't understand this until now, I decide I won't try anymore to understand it, and move on does good to perspective. Important truths very few people agree with me: Don't die. Everyday doctors to be substituted by AI in short years. Social skills will likewise be dominated by AI.

Math.title: optimizer for gradient descent
Math.content:
to chat gpt:

in the gradient descent algorithm I was wondering what would be the most efficient learning rate. What I got to, and want you opinion on, is that thinking of the step after getting the gradient vector, what fundamentally will happen, in the limit you go with super small learning rate, is you will trace out the path descending on the direction of largest decrease until that changes. So the most efificnet learning path would be, in terms of steps, to go down as far as possible as loing as the direction in which you are going down aligns with the opposite of gradient, and right there change to the next. The minorant on the number of steps would be the number of direction changes. Now of course for continuous functions directions can change just a bit, so we'd have to be guaranteed that the small deviations on the path, if there are any, and in general there are, dont take us away from the next "milestone" per say. So if you have to fix a step then it would be that which minimizes the aggregate of these steps. For example the smallest of them, as in the smallest distance between points where gradient significantly changes. That is an example, it doesnt generalize as the best procedure for all cases but is a start

Math.title: The Selfish Gene: The Futility of Perfect Strategies
Math.content: "Quando os lemingues fogem aos milhões do centro de uma explosão populacional, não o fazem para reduzir a densidade da área que abandonaram, mas sim pelo propósito muito egoísta de encontrarem mais espaço para viverem. O facto de qualquer lemingue em particular poder nunca chegar a encontrar esse espaço e morrer é algo que poderemos vir a verificar. Mas isso não altera que, ficando para trás, seria maior a probabilidade de essa ser a pior aposta." Está a ficar mais clara uma das coisas que já pensava: quando se adota uma estratégia intermédia (a virtude perfeita), como há sempre uma dissipação desta perfeição, vamos andar a boiar. Os indivíduos de uma população, guiados pelos genes egoístas, vão tentar arranjar táticas para ganhar à tática dos outros. E assim sucessivamente. É como jogar pedra, papel, tesoura. Ele joga tesoura e eu pedra. Por isso eu penso: "ele agora vai jogar papel porque eu joguei pedra e ganhei. Por isso jogo tesoura. Ah não, ele está a pensar exatamente nisso, por isso vai jogar pedra, então jogo papel. Mas ele também pensou assim..." Como as táticas não são 100% firmes, podemos jogar com elas e os outros com as nossas. É como aquilo do "eu sei que tu sabes; e o outro diz, eu sei que tu sabes que eu sei." O problema é que as táticas extremistas, embora aparentemente incorruptíveis (a ideia do Batman, por exemplo) nunca trarão liberdade. E então as táticas dependem também das táticas que os outros usam. Se todos usam uma tática, essa deixa de ser lá grande coisa.

Math.title: The Selfish Gene: Evolutionary Game Theory and Unpredictability
Math.content: Quanto mais similar és com outro, mais competição surgirá, porque lutarão pelos mesmos recursos, terão os mesmos interesses e a mesma vontade de propagar a sua informação genética. Seja na guerra do desgaste, ou noutro tipo de guerra, quanto mais imprevisível fores, melhor. Normalmente nas disputas entre espécies oscila-se de entre um e Equilíbrio (o tal swing) porque uma usa uma estratégia nova que se espalha e domina, mas de repente muitos usam essa estratégia e por isso outra que seja relativamente melhor que essa vai dominar, e assim sucessivamente. Para dominar outra, há que encontrar a melhor estratégia, e que essa mesma contenha a regra de mudar a estratégia, e assim sucessivamente. A melhor estratégia é completamente definida não pelo seu conteúdo, mas sim pelo seu contexto.

Math.title: Alex in Numberland: Language, Infinity, and the Normal Distribution
Math.content: It's just so cool that there is so much to counting, and how language affects it. How we say numbers shows our past, our stuff. Interesting that talking about origami, there was a guy who instead of making origami to make figures, just made it to find math theorems and stuff of that nature. It's like analytical geometry to geometry. He talks about Indians, and their names for numbers. And the freaking Buddha knows numbers (in factors of 100) until 10 to the 421. It's of utter ridiculousness that he knows numbers until this, and the author points out that this obsession with the bigger and smaller numbers is metaphysical in nature. Since pi seems to be the norm, in that it has a normal distribution, it is a sort of predetermined randomness, so it would be perfect for stuff that is better without patterns, such as deciding your poker move or stuff like that.

Math.title: Alex in Numberland: Base 12, Cardinality, and Meta Numbers
Math.content: Some people say a 12-number based system is actually more natural and better than the 10 we have. And this is legit insane, it's mind-boggling to think that it is a man-made construct that ten is the end of the digits. And it's because of fingers and hands, goddamn fingers and hands. Then I started thinking, wait, but ten multiplied by any number is just adding a zero, but with base twelve what happens? Cause that was sort of my justification for why we use base 10, it seems obvious, because I never thought of the possibility of 12 being the end of digits. And then I was like, wait, but does ten hold these properties in the 12 system? It should, it's the number, not the fact that it is the last digit, that makes it have that property. But then what interesting mathematical facts are there in alternative number systems, like in arithmetic and stuff? Like there can be a study of alternative number systems, and the properties for the number systems of base belonging to R, or even negative numbers, who cares. It's meta numbers study. And this is such a new thought, it's something that I had never thought about. And this makes the distinction between ordinality and cardinality make sense. Cause it's not the order that makes the properties, it's the cardinality, the quantity. You can have number 100 on the duodecimal system, but that is not the number who has the properties of 100. Since I had only thought about one system, for me ordinality and cardinality were blended. How have I never conceived this contingency? It's fucking wow. And twelve can be better because of divisibility. It makes it easier because there are more integer portions. It makes multiplication tables more periodic, it even includes the pattern of 3, 6, 9, so maybe Tesla was referring indirectly to this thing of 12. And in reality hours are in 12: dozens, months, inches.

Math.title: Alex in Numberland: Logarithmic Perception and Exponential Growth
Math.content: We actually have a logarithmic perception of the world, our way of measuring stuff in our terms is logarithmic, as the indigenous show. And that explains that time gets faster as we age. We measure in ratios, and I would say psychology plays a big role in this, since our perception of more being less over repetition affects our behavior which affects our perception down the pipeline. We sort of develop internal measuring mechanisms, standard measures. For example, ants that had their legs cut and put longer ones, would overshoot the distance; they measure by steps. We also measure with the circadian rhythm... And this ties back to the memory thing, to which I have an analogy: it's like a typewriter that writes, but as time goes on just stands by, drawing a line instead of writing. In sum, we need exponential growth/evolution to counter our natural tendency, and maybe that is why we have trouble understanding deeply exponential growth. Our ratios get played. This way of counting ratios in approximation, in a way, is a consequence of the error being a matter of ratio, and to minimize the error one should maximize ratio intuition. You won't get exact answers, but pretty much every time you will give an accurate range.

Math.title: The Master Algorithm: Induction as the Inverse of Deduction
Math.content: Afirma que a indução é o inverso da dedução, e que na história, quando procuramos inversos de operações conhecidas, descobrimos coisas novas e relevantes. Para tornar certas contas de aritmética possíveis (tipo 4-7), sacaram-se os números inteiros; com a divisão, números racionais; com as raízes de negativos, números complexos. O outro exemplo que dá é: Sócrates humano, humano mortal, logo Sócrates mortal. E na indução temos o inverso. Sócrates humano, Sócrates mortal, o que falta? Falta fazer uma generalização, neste caso pode ser que todos os humanos são mortais. Newton formulou leis da indução, e uma delas é "tudo o que for verdade para tudo o que já vimos é verdade para tudo no Universo".

Math.title: The Book of Why: Causeless Correlation and the Monty Hall Deception
Math.content: "Causal assumptions cannot be invented at our whim, they can be falsified." "Causeless correlation violates our common sense." Basicamente, a transferência de informação sem causalidade é o que cria a correlação. O collider bias é estranho. O aumento de probabilidade a partir do Teorema de Bayes sem que haja uma comunicação foge à nossa intuição. "(Monty Hall) uses our own cognitive machinery to deceive us." This also has to do with backdoor information propagation. "Whereas regularities can be observed, counterfactuals can only be imagined." "To me formulas are baked ideas. Written words are ideas in the oven" - analogy with Bertrand Russell.

Literature.title: O Alienista: O Formato da Obra e a Ironia de Machado
Literature.content: Quanto ao livro em si, penso que é assumidamente conciso, e assumidamente porque diz o autor na conversa com o narrador que o desfecho é de tal ordem e tão inesperado que merecia nada menos que 10 capítulos de exposição, mas que o autor se contenta com 1. O Machado de Assis aguentou a respiração. Um português estranho, a caminhar na fronteira do estrangeiro, e também parece velho, tem ali uns traços, umas rugas que indicam isso. Imaginem que tínhamos de escolher um livro para todos lermos. Para satisfazer igualmente a vontade de todos, podíamos fazer um livro grande e mau, o que acham? Ou um livro pequeno e bom, e cá está ele. É suficientemente leve para não se encalhar, mas suficientemente pesado para nos fazer parar para pensar. Espero que com esta apresentação tenha conseguido passar a mesma ironia e tom humorístico que o livro me passou. É interessante que a história é contada como se o autor a tivesse escrito e estivesse a lê-la para nós; ele já sabe o que vai acontecer e conta-nos, tal como eu o faço. Epah eu hoje acordei a pensar que era cómico, e o ser humano tem de pensar assim. Eu na minha cabeça sou o mais cómico, mesmo que não seja.

Literature.title: O Alienista: O Perfeito Desequilíbrio e o Cativeiro de Si Próprio
Literature.content: Mas, vá lá, Simão entende que estava errado, que a loucura estava afinal na perfeição, e que ser normal era atingir o perfeito desequilíbrio. O que é certo é que as pessoas são libertadas do cativeiro da Casa Verde, e até o barbeiro ganancioso decidiu que preferia a glória da tesoura à governação de Itaguaí. Epah, está tudo louco, só pode. E pronto, viveu tudo feliz para s... oh não, esperem aí. Se pensam que Simão está acabado então ainda não conhecem o nosso alienista. Nota de seguida que no meio daquilo tudo é ele quem tem o equilíbrio perfeito das faculdades. Ele é afinal o primeiro exemplo da sua nova teoria (a tese levou à antítese que deu na síntese). Fecha-se na Casa Verde e vai-se observar através da tela áspera da ciência, quiçá curar-se. Acaba por morrer 1 ano mais tarde.

Literature.title: O Alienista: A Expansão da Loucura e a Revolta dos Barbeiros
Literature.content: Mas o alienista quer mais, e chega a uma nova teoria, em que a sanidade é o perfeito equilíbrio das faculdades mentais, e tudo o resto é loucura. O estoque de amostras na Casa Verde multiplica-se, a tal ponto que 80 por cento da população está lá. Até o padre confessa que não sabia que havia assim tantos loucos no mundo. É tudo suave e bom até que a pessoa mais popular de Itaguaí lá vai parar. Um que tinha a mania de cumprimentar toda a gente. Qualquer idiossincrasia é digna de estudo, por isso cuidadinho com o que dizem porque o alienista vai-te pegar. Nem a mulher está safa, porque a ciência é imparcial. Começa-se a fazer sentir o ressoar de insatisfação no povo. Uma pessoa conspirou que seria inveja do Simão, e isso mereceu-lhe um lugar na galeria dos alucinados. As pessoas acabam por revoltar-se, e lideradas por um barbeiro, vão ao encontro do alienista. O barbeiro almeja chegar ao poder, e é mesmo aclamado de governador. No entanto, chegado ao poder, aproveita o alienista como um eliminador dos seus adversários políticos, o que leva o outro barbeiro da cidade a revoltar-se e tornar-se governador. Assim sendo, aprendemos que é preciso ter atenção aos barbeiros.

Literature.title: O Alienista: A Ciência Acima de Tudo e a Casa Verde
Literature.content: O livro de que vos falo foi escrito em 82 do século XIX, por Machado de Assis. A única nota inicial de autor é uma onde discute a utilização do vocábulo "reprochar", se é ou não um francesismo, e se soa bem. Enfim, coisas dele. A história é sobre Simão Bacamarte, um psiquiatra. Acredita que a ciência (ou pseudociência) é o seu emprego único, e Itaguaí o seu Universo. Faz tudo pela ciência e com a ciência. Por exemplo, casou com uma mulher que nem é bonita nem é simpática, mas que reúne as condições fisiológicas para ter bons filhos. Simão observa que naquele sítio ninguém cura os loucos. Devido a esta má exploração da psiquiatria, ambiciona ele criar um sítio, a Casa Verde, para onde vão todos os loucos, onde possa estudá-los e curá-los, a fim de desvendar a natureza da loucura e descobrir o remédio universal. E começam a chegar os considerados doidinhos. Um tal de João de Deus que diz ser o Deus João, que está convencido que se falar o mundo acaba; outro que se põe a fazer discursos académicos depois de todos os almoços.

Social.title: Human, All Too Human: Selfless Revolutionaries and Reverse Psychology
Social.content: That selfless revolutionaries are more dangerous than selfish ones. In my opinion, the first ones have faith, and so their belief is much more fixed and independent of criteria, unnegotiable. For the latter however, no matter how deeply rooted they are, there is always a good enough condition that will make them step over their belief. To use reverse psychology with the masses, which Nietzsche calls getting benefit from the counterpassion that is awakened by you emphasizing a certain passion. Gives the example that if you didn't wanted France to be more catholic as a German then you would hate Catholicism in Germany. Give them a stance so they can counter, because at the end of the day the business of war relies in finding reasons to explain the reciprocal hate, in the minds of the naive. That the recipe for the great man of the masses is simple, he just has to be relatable and that in order to achieve something pleasant he uses an extraordinary will. So he has to achieve something pleasant and seem to have a great will. He then says, and this is the real hot sauce for which I wrote the last paragraph, that everyone admires will because no one has it, and everybody tells themselves that if they had it there would be no limits for them.

Social.title: Human, All Too Human: Winning People, Adapting, and the Ego of the Reader
Social.content: That there is always this partisan in a party that everyone inside the party demises. This shows your flaws in your presentation, for some people took it in ways they shouldn't have, and now he is a force against your partisan, for when he speaks he hammers the party's ideas. This force serves as a way to see how the fundamentalists of that party will be like. Most hilarious is that, a la Incredibles with Incrediboy, mostly these guys that end up screwing this up are the pedestalizers, the ones that idolized so much became so much fans that they became blind to other ways. They seem to be the perfect candidate, for they were born and grown around the party, and yet they come to love in such a way that it is even suspicious even to the very creators for they don't think they have found the ultimate recipe. Winning people over is also about appealing to what they want. Persuading a brave person to do something means placing greater emphasis on the danger and risk associated with said action, if it's a person of altruism, bring that up, if it's a person moved by a fight against injustice, activate those buttons inside their mind by ringing up the volume of the correspondent knots. Since you have all these ways of adapting your personality, conduct, to the situation you are in, you then get stuck some but to that, so when you come back to the places, even tho you got way beyond that, it's harder to bring that new you to the old places. A paragraph that could be added: 1345 - Ego of the reader. There exist brave enough writers to want their points understood. Some readers find it, however, an insult to their time and their finer intellect.

Science.title: The Unabomber Manifesto: Science as a Surrogate Activity
Science.content: Under this paradigm, says Ted, scientists are not there for curiosity, or to benefit humanity, it's their surrogate activity, it's the way they find of going through their power process. Yep that is a factor, but how do they choose to work on a specific thing? Following this reasoning it seems that what they develop in their head as the best thing is the one which gives them fulfillment, and so they go for it. Usually the one which is the best is the one that is scarce, and therefore, usually hard or inutile. So hard things give more fulfillment to scientists and others of course.

Science.title: The Meaning of It All: The Atomic Hypothesis and Fundamental Truths
Science.content: Feynman diz que a frase que conveys the most information in the least words about the world is the atomic hypothesis. That if it were passed onto the next generation of creatures, it would pass the most info. Maybe it's because it's the most true thing we know, but probably not. More because it is the most fundamental thing that we are pretty confident we know. But would it be the best thing we could pass to the next generation? Would they get the most out of this and not other pieces of information? In a sense, it's hard to imagine that another thing could be the most important. Essentially, what everything is at its core is the most revealing truth, the biggest pocket of reducibility.
